### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-09
With the release of 11.10 & the inclusion of gnome-shell in the official repos gNatty has outlived it's usefulness. 

This project is dead, torrents removed from linuxtracker.org 

Don't know about the rest of you but I learned a lot doing this. * 30 Dec 2011


If you found this thread as a result of Andrew's article on [Webupd8]("http://www.webupd8.org/2011/07/gnatty-ubuntu-1104-remaster-with-gnome.html#more"), thanks for checking it out & welcome to the project.  Please re-seed since that is the only current distribution method.  If anyone has a site to host it for reliable direct downloading that would be great too.

For maximum stability I also recommend that you disable ricotz/testing ppa immediately after installation.  If you like to tweak things until they break I recommend frequent backups, I like Clonezilla.

Recent updates may have broken a couple extensions, if you are unable to login to your Gnome 3.0 shell desktop I recommend that you download the most recent gNatty Extensions, link is near bottom of this post. (Make note of your weather settings if you replace that extension)  I have made some modifications to a couple of them so they no longer seem to cause a problem & continue to cooperate with each other. * 29 June 2011

[ATTACH]195742[/ATTACH]

32-bit version released 22 June 2011, gNatty Gnome i686 v3.1 Live
64-bit version released 16 June 2011, gNatty Gnome amd64 v3 Live
[COLOR=Red](Download links at bottom of this post)[/COLOR]

**Overview:**

This is a basic, stock Ubuntu 11.04 installation & live iso with the inclusion of the latest Gnome 3.0 packages constructed with UCK. It is based on the UGR (Ubuntu Gnome Remix) project, with the addition of the rictotz/testing ppa and comes with themes & extensions to be installed by the user.  Despite what some people think, Gnome 3.0 shell & Unity can coexist in the same installation.


**Installation Tips:**
Attempting to update a previous version of Ubuntu is not recommended, this should be used as a new installation. (Pretty standard disclaimer)

1. If you have problems loading try selecting **f6** from the grub screen and select nomodeset.

2. Read & follow the Installation Notes.

3. If everything installed ok you should now have the option of selecting between Ubuntu GNOME Shell (Gnome), Classic Gnome w/o effects, Classic Gnome, & Ubuntu (Unity).

[B]
General Notes:[/B]
Please be as generous as possible seeding this file, my connection is painfully slow so it needs all the help it can get.

Feel free to ask any question you want.

[B][COLOR=Indigo]The recipe:

The majority of the formula comes from the UGR (Ubuntu Gnome Remix) Project so I'll point you to their website for instructions, my only real change, other than activating a couple optional repositories is to add this PPA:  [COLOR=Black]**sudo apt-add-repository ppa:ricotz/testing**[/COLOR] before the updating & upgrading.[/COLOR][/B]

[[COLOR=Red]Ubuntu Gnome Remix (UGR) Project installation instructions[/COLOR]]("http://ugr.teampr0xy.net/install")


Let's have fun!

Error message on boot in Live Session - Post #32
Restore gdm as default login - Post #33

Introduced gNatty Pack - Post #64
Removing Nvidia driver after gNatty installation - Post #67
Using nomodeset on boot - Post #72
Direct download link - Post #74
Installing clone of gNatty on system with RAID array - Post #89
Explanation/description of extensions in gNatty Pack - Post #782
Good format for requesting help - #484
(work in progress)

**Download links:**

Hey good work!

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-10
> **satanselbow said:**
> Hey good work!

I'm just having a play with the mini.iso / G3 ppa and the perfectminimal script to try and get a gnome-shell mini one on the go ;)

Have you given up on remastersys altogether? And any top tips for getting the best out of UCK?

Kudos my friend kudos :popcorn:

I've thought about trying the mini.iso again, but I think I'll wait until I get a faster connection.

No, haven't given up on Remastersys altogether, I may dust it off again.  No real tips for UCK, it's really straight forward. Basically what it does is mount the iso you want for a base, then mounts the squash.fs into it's own little environment.  From there you have a couple options, native language, remove languages, remove windows files (wubi & crap).  Then you have the option of using a terminal console or synaptic, you can alternate back & forth, it's not an all or nothing proposition. What I have been doing is using both to add ppa's, updates & upgrades.  Remove packages, install drivers, etc..  When you get done with that you just click another button and it reassembles an iso for you containing all the changes.  For someone that really knows how to use it they may be able to add files, change defaults, edit scripts, but I'm not certain of that.

As an example, the iso I created today is absolutely up to date as of this past afternoon.  It contains one of the latest Nvidia & ATI video drivers installed.  I added ubuntu tweak, synapse & IIRC xfce4-panel (it makes a nice vertical dock among other things), also included the minimal LXDE desktop that was pulled in with lxdm.

I don't know if it's going to work or not but I'm in the process of trying to remaster LMDE (Linux Mint Debian Edition) with gnome 3 installed, though anything might happen considering I have to dip into their experimental repositories to get it.  Might not build, or it might do nothing. :)

I'm having fun though, this has got me using software that I didn't even know existed a couple weeks ago.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-10
I noticed that the installer is the same as the Natty one .......

So I installed it using >>>  Something Else

which was the 3rd option down on my screen ......

Then when it gets past the Location Keyboard settings ...... and checks the drives connected to the system

I then choose the area I have previously set up using gparted ...... ( thats just me though ) 

A routine I find hard to break ...... 
I always set the partitions up prior to any install .....

Then just point to the correct partition and change the bot drive >>> which always appears under the
partition listing in the installer ...... its a drop down menu ..... and you pick where you want 
the boot partition to be ........


After that you pretty much just leave it to do its thing ......

I get a error about the Session after boot ........ which is the same one we get from the live CD

It continues past this though and then works alright ,,,,,,

I should imagine if any people get it dropping to Classic on the old laptop from (live CD mode) and into

LXDE on my desktop (installed) after the error just do ALT + F2 

gnome-shell --replace 

A graphics driver issue that needs sorting ...... will possibly drop it to Classic or LXDE

I saw that error on the old laptop I used too ...... but on that one it dropped to the Classic mode ....
but thinking about it - I was also running from the Live CD  ..... so thats why Classic mode
The other was from the Install and that drops into LXDE ...... but Gnome Shell WORKS

just by doing ALT + F2

**gnome-shell --replace**

or you can log out (now its installed) and change sessions as you would normally from the login screen

Quite a few choices already set up there , and this is pretty fast too on an older system ......

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-10
> **YesWeCan said:**
> So I shrank my Windows partition to make 70GB for gNatty.
Unfortunately, the gNatty installer won't allow me to install to my IDE disk partition, sda2. It recognizes my other 4 SATA disks but I don't want to use any of these. Curiously, it allows me to select /dev/sda for Grub.

Did you create a swap partition, or does one already exist?  I don't usually use a swap partition but I noticed during the alpha/beta stage with Natty that it demanded one or it would not install.  I just created a small one & haven't had any problems since.

Something I do on my system is to create fairly small linux partitions for installation and separate partitions for my Downloads & Music, etc.. then I use mnt/bind in fstab to mount them in my home folders.  So I have about 8-9 linux installations that all share "folders". (I do a lot of installations and don't like shuffling things around)

> 

BTW, is it possible to put in a decent installer program? I want to have complete control over Grub and I want it to explain everything it intends to do and get my confirmation before it does it.

Unfortunately it's not, creating or adapting installers I'll leave up to the mother distro.

You could skip installing the loader, or install the loader to the install partition, though it will give some warnings like that, especially grub 1.99~, however they are more informative than critical.

> 
It also reports a System Problem after booting the USB and after I quit the installer it reported unexpected Gnome shell shutdown. It still seems to carry on working, however.

Yes, there is a small little bug, but it doesn't seem critical. Once it's installed you should be able to just do a **sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade** to bring everything up to date cure that.  You can try **sudo apt-get install -f** if a package is stubborn about upgrading.

It sounds from what you said earlier that you have a few hard drives ..... do you have
a USB hard drive .... as I find these invaluable for trying new things out on ....

Its just a case of unplugging one and plugging another in to go from system to system
and on each of mine I also have a choice of many systems too .....

Just a thought as they are a safe-ish way to install and keep separate from other
things ..... just unplug when you do not need them ..... 

I have 4 now and the prices keep dropping for these too ..... the Seagate 500Gig ones
are good IMHO ....  just a thought .

( If you use Windows a lot still ..... there lies a problem .... setting the boot up is never going to be easy )

My worry was always losing Windows as there were a few programs I thought I needed ..... but once I did a 
whole new World opened up and I really did start to enjoy Linux.

If Windows is a concern ..... try a 500Gig USB drive out ..... you should not be disappointed.

Other wise some one like [COLOR=Red]**[Old Fred]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=852711") or [Drs305]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=223945")**[/COLOR]  are the ones that appear to know more about the boot process than most ...... 
check any of there threads out .... you will see some good answers to awkward problems for booting grub2 and problems 
with disk structures too.

A good page to checkout is here >>> [COLOR=Red]**[LINK]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1195275")**[/COLOR]

Good suggestions. I have a couple of WD USB drives but I use them for backups.

I am a Ubuntu convert. I normally run Lynx off a RAID10 array. I keep Win7 on its own 160GB disk for emergency use and I just created a 70GB ext4 partition on it and a 4GB swap for gNatty. The trouble was that the USB stick gNatty installer would not see the 160GB drive (sda). It would see my 4 RAID drives. Why - I have no idea. So this prevented me from using the installer. Instead, I just rsync'd the USB root directory and children to the HD partition and then manually booted it using my Lynx Grub bootloader (and I have now added it to the menu).

So far so good. I had to create a user for myself and then I disabled the root user. I had to set the language up. Update Manager installed 31 updates. Things seem to work "as if" I had done a proper install process.

What I am wondering is what does the Ubuntu installer do that I haven't done? :-s

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-10
Not completely sure on whether or not this will work or not .......we did it before ....

but not sure what will happen or if it will work - but there is one way to find out I guess

if you want to .......

[LEFT]**sudo apt-get install ppa-purge**
[/LEFT]
 [B]sudo ppa-purge ppa:gnome3-team/gnome3
[/B]sudo add-apt-repository [B]ppa:gnome3-team/gnome3
[/B][LEFT]**sudo apt-get update**
[/LEFT]
**sudo apt-get install aptitude**
[B]sudo aptitude update
[/B][B]sudo aptitude install gir1.2-mutter-3.0
[/B][B]sudo aptitude install gnome-session

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-10
> **YesWeCan said:**
> 

What I am wondering is what does the Ubuntu installer do that I haven't done? :-s

Probably nothing, other than give locale & partitioning options.  Sounds like you're running a HDD version of a USB live.

But if it let you create users, update, etc.. it sounds like you do have a "real" installation to me.

As for the SATA thing, my guess is that there is a bug, maybe in mdadm(?) that is disabling SATA and giving preference to your array.  Maybe not a bug?  Perhaps done on purpose to make sure people don't inadvertently destroy their array, though anyone running an array is probably experienced enough not to hose their system. :)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-10
> **23dornot23d said:**

re-install or set-up from the CD now ..... so no real problem.

Have fun ..... ;)

No, unlike using Remastersys I couldn't figure out how to pre-install that stuff.  The 2d link of my signature will take you to the gNatty Pack (tell me if it doesn't work, it's just SkyDrive).  My primitive solution was to create a tarball of my ~/.themes & ~/.local/share/gnome-shell/extensions folders.  Also included is the gnome-shell-user-themes-extension~.deb. I've made a couple tweaks to the themes & theme selector, there are 8 functional shell themes in there, I modified Atolm for a narrower top panel.  Theme selector displays 4 columns instead of the original 3.  For the extensions I compressed each one separately, just place them in the extensions folder and extract the ones you want to use.  Also edited the metadata.json to make them a little more forgiving for build versions, I may have missed one or two.  The edit consisted of simply changing version from something like 3.0.1 to simply 3.0 so minor revision changes would stop breaking the extension.

Basically just trying to consolidate some of the tweaks I've made over the past month or so.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-11
@waspbloke

> 
Anyway, unfortuantely I have to report that both my ATI laptops (a 32  and 64) won't play nicely. I haven't completely run out of ideas yet so  I'll save asking for help for the moment, although I'm pretty sure my  6yo lappy just won't get there. My nice posh 64bit Dell really should be  able to squeeze some sort of usable system out tho' so I'll persevere  with that. Any general recommendations appreciated.
Back with it again ..... the 

[[COLOR=Red]**64 bit**[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1750450") one you need to install HTOP

sudo apt-get install HTOP 

....... and kill off the greeter ...... wait for about 30 secs or so and it will restart gdm ..... press unlock on the login screen and you are into Gnome Shell   ........

___________________________________________

The 32 bit ..... I had to just use nomodeset on the first screen from live CD to get it to boot ok  ..... 
Then as it boots .....
click on the message that pops up about a problem with sessions ,,,, and it should go straight in ......

Once installed ...... the only error message again should be the Session one ........

___________________________________________

Onward ...... just got the 32 bit onto my twin screen ..... 

All I have really done so far is to ....... start up LXDE ...... change the background to
the Tron Theme from earlier  in this thread ....

then

add kdm
sudo apt-get install kdm

add cairo-dock
sudo aptitude install cairo-dock

______________________________________

Now to get the Themes sorted out ......

ok I am going to try to it this way ..... first ...

aptitude install libgtk-3-dev

we have dependency issues .... so I chose to downgrade

> 
Accept this solution? [Y/n/q/?] n
The following actions will resolve these dependencies:

     Downgrade the following packages:                              
1)     libxi6 [2:1.4.1-1ubuntu2.1 (now) -> 2:1.4.1-1ubuntu2 (natty)]



Accept this solution? [Y/n/q/?] y
The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:
  libxi6 
The following NEW packages will be installed:
  debhelper{a} html2text{a} libatk1.0-dev{a} 

etc .......... but all installed now

Ok we now have themes ..... and we do need to install using the Alien Converted packages
sudo apt-get install gdebi-core

[COLOR=Red]**and you have to be root to do it otherwise it fails ........**[/COLOR] [B]remember to reboot too
[/B] 
gdebi gnome-shell-extensions-common_3.0.1-2_all.deb
gdebi gnome-shell-extensions-user-theme_3.0.0-6.6_all.deb

  ;)
The themes are unpacked into a Themes folder in   /.local/share/gnome-shell/

Are you using an older or newer version of the theme selector?  The one I'm using, and the one in the gNatty Pack expect the shell & themes in ~/.themes.  Gnome-tweak-tool uses that same directory for metacity themes as well.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-11
> **cecilpierce said:**
> question:
I had desktop effects on ubuntu and ubuntu classic and for some reason they only work on classic.
Any idea what happen to ubuntu and compiz, when I open compiz in ubuntu I can't make any checks.

When you write ubuntu do you mean unity?  I'm guessing it's related to the way video is being detected in some way.  You could try using **Additional Drivers** to see what drivers are activated &/or installed.

In gNatty I may have inadvertently introduced a problem by trying to package both the Nvidia & ATI driver.  Purge, remove, uninstall the one that doesn't apply.  You may have to use synaptic to completely remove it.

Another thing to try is re-installing compiz.

If you are doing frequent upgrades it's also possible that things got temporarily broken somehow.

Try exploring these suggestions and let us know how it goes.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-11
sorted the moz problem ..... with

root@keith-MS-7181:/usr/lib# ln -s /usr/lib/libmozjs185.so.1.0.0 /usr/lib/libmozjs.so

after loading the libmozjs185-1.0 in from synaptic ..... also loaded the libmozjs185-dev one in too

Basically pick the ones that have the cube on and resolve any issues .....

to get the above install as below .... I always add it to cairo-dock too ..... for easy access ....

sudo apt-get install compizconfig-settings-manager


Just to keep this uptodate ..... I did a 

aptitude update
aptitude safe-uprade

```

root@keith-MS-7181:/home/keith# aptitude safe-upgrade
Resolving dependencies...                
No packages will be installed, upgraded, or removed.
0 packages upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 2 not upgraded.
Need to get 0 B of archives. After unpacking 0 B will be used.
                                         
root@keith-MS-7181:/home/keith# 


```Fully uptodate as of tonight and Classic runs ..... Unity runs ..... Gnome-Shell runs .... LXDE runs
same Session ......

One more thing ... I always replace the Desktop manager startup Login with >>> kdm

works for me .....

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-12
Do NOT install the gnome3-session package.  I can't remember exactly why not but do know it will break things.

I remember now, there are 4 developmental &/or testing repos that gNatty pulls the Gnome 3.0 files from.  I did this to keep things as current as possible since I don't know which is the most active.  Pretty sure the majority come from the UGR guys (ubuntugnometeam), but a couple packages come from ricotz/testing, and possibly a couple from the remaining two.  Your system is dependent on the ugr-desktop-g3 package and I'm pretty sure that installing gnome3-session will break that.  If you want to test it though it can probably be re-installed, I haven't tried it.

### Post by satanselbow on 2011-05-12
Hi kids :D

Only 1 minimal install - I've done about 30 in the last couple of days :D

The minimal installed is really just an ugly netinstall - as i'm sure you've found out...

The trick to keep Unity and as much Gnome2 stuff out the frame as possible is to use the "manual package selection" option right at the bottom of the list on the Tasksel screen - ie where you get to choose whether you want Ubuntu Server, Print Server etc.

You will end up with a very very minimal Ubuntu install with no GUI - just a login prompt.

I've done so many possibilities in the last 48h and documented it so badly :D

1st steps are thus:

Enables add-apt-repository - 
```
apt-get python-software-properties
```Add Gnome3-Team PPA
```
add-apt-repository ppa:gnome3-team/gnome3
apt-get update
```Very basic bits that get you a G3 Shell (all get pulled from g3 team ppa)
```
gdm gnome-shell gnome-session
```
And if you want to login to a session that does nothing at all... er... right...
```
sudo service gdm start
```:popcorn::popcorn::popcorn::popcorn:

I am still kind of in dependancy hell sifting through what has to go on in what order - BUT am compiling a bash script as I go :D

( ty for letting me know your experiences with it though ....... )

_________________________________________

Imagine this ....

You get your Distro ....

3 questions ....

1 ....... What Graphics Card do you use ...... Intel / Nvidia / ATI ( default to SAFE MODE if user is unsure )

ok now we have something for you to look at ......

2 ...... How do you connect to the Internet ...... A cable ..... USB / ETHO ....... or Wireless ...... ( default to CABLE if user is unsure )


3 ..... Do you want a Classic Desktop / Gnome-Shell ........ or the UNITY one ....... or a BLANK one to add DOCKS / Panels TO ...... ( default to CLASSIC if user is unsure )


Now from here we can help any user build a system ....... if they have a Graphical User Interface (GUI) .... INTERNET  and a workspace they are familiar with .......


Where we go now is up to you the USER ....... we just have to think of a way to supply you with a list of desktop looks to choose from 

 >>> that suit YOU ......

just a thought ....... but I think its possible to do this .........

__________________________________________________________

Nothing should stop you creating the look you want now ...... all the pieces are there to use ......

( add your applications using the Software Center or Synaptic .... or apt-get or aptitude ..... or choose a default set )

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-12
> **satanselbow said:**
> Hi kids :D

Only 1 minimal install - I've done about 30 in the last couple of days :D

The minimal installed is really just an ugly netinstall - as i'm sure you've found out...

The trick to keep Unity and as much Gnome2 stuff out the frame as possible is to use the "manual package selection" option right at the bottom of the list on the Tasksel screen - ie where you get to choose whether you want Ubuntu Server, Print Server etc.

You will end up with a very very minimal Ubuntu install with no GUI - just a login prompt.

I've done so many possibilities in the last 48h and documented it so badly :D

1st steps are thus:

Enables add-apt-repository - 
```
apt-get python-software-properties
```Add Gnome3-Team PPA
```
add-apt-repository ppa:gnome3-team/gnome3
apt-get update
```Very basic bits that get you a G3 Shell (all get pulled from g3 team ppa)
```
gdm gnome-shell gnome-session
```
And if you want to login to a session that does nothing at all... er... right...
```
sudo service gdm start
```:popcorn::popcorn::popcorn::popcorn:

I am still kind of in dependancy hell sifting through what has to go on in what order - BUT am compiling a bash script as I go :D

The one I built last night used the ugr-desktop-g3, which is totally complete from what I could tell.  I pulled from 4 active gnome3 PPAs, it works well but it's not small.  My original thought process was that I would have an installation of nothing but gnome 3.0, to include fallback mode, and nothing more.

### Post by philnice on 2011-05-12
....And here i'm.... So, i tried every available alternative regarding the graphics driver, including building them form source:
-In the case of ati (open source driver), the system did not boot the gnome shell even once. Always in fallback mode. -The problem with the firefox lag  did not appeared though. I think it wouldn't unless if there was some sort of fglrx presenting. 
-In the case of fglrx: i only tested the one i builted from source - for obvious reasons - and the results were exactly the same when i was using the one provided by the distro: The shell would load as soon as the fglrx (any kind) was installed, but that would result in  a system lagginess especially when firefox was running.

After the last, i decided to purge both fglrx and open source and start over. But the kernel did not boot after.
So, now im using my other partition - ubuntu 10.10 with the old gnome ppa and an incomplete shell, which brings me to the point:** On this system i also use the fglrx, and there is no sign of lag or anything anywhere including firefox (which i use right now.).** Also, the shell have its effects. Considering all of that, i think there should be some sort of conflicts between gnome 3 and unity, but to be sure i would have to use a minimal install ( like the one mentioned above), with only gnome3 - shell installed and nothing else regarding the DE.
Also, this way i could confirm that my claim is valid for the latest 2.38 kernel also.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-12
> **satanselbow said:**
> Oh I see ;) I missed a lot of the Gnome 3 dev cycle as had a lot of bill paying work on at the time.

I've had everything **dis-functional **quicte a lot recently :D

You know all this is quite ridiculous really... Unity is a hacked - or "custom" if you are feeling generous - Gnome 3 right? So somewhere in the bowels of the Canonical ISO Library there must be a plain vanilla Natty+ G3... You know they could just give it to us tomorrow - and still beat Fedora/OpenSuse past the post... I smell a conspiracy :D

IIRC, to get that "clean" look it was just adding gnome-shell, not the updated gnome-session. The way they have been developing things lately I don't think it's possible to separate the two anymore (as soon as I write this somebody will prove me wrong).

BTW, after playing with a minimal install I've reached the conclusion that the best, and simplest method is to use the UGR repos in addition to gnome3-team & ricotz, just install the ugr-desktop-g3 and avoid the dependency hell.  After which you can purge unity* and a few other things you don't want.  Not saying it can't be done the way you are doing it, just that I think you will create problems for yourself down the road.  That's my opinion at this time.

You are probably right about the link between unity & gnome 3.0, there are too many similarities and Ubuntu definitely copied (poorly) the look and feel of the new shell.  For a team of programmers removing dependency on mutter and replacing with compiz & changing the programming language is probably not that difficult.  Again, that's my opinion, since I don't program I may be way off base. :)

The problem with multiple icons for the same app on the dash is the same age old gnome problem of having multiple categories per app. Think Evolution appearing as "Internet" and "Office" for the email and calendar respectively. ### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-12
> **waspbloke said:**
> Okay, themes working now as per gNatty pack instructions. But now sidebar icons and a few other elements have turned into coloured noise/static...!!!!


Ok, they did an update on gnome-shell (I think it was) the other day and it caused some weirdness in some themes, though what you're seeing might be ATI related.

Open up gnome-tweak-tool and double check some of the themes selections.  In Gnome 3.0 you can change Shell themes, metacity, GTK & icon themes.

I think there is some conflict between the way gnome-tweak-tool does themes & the way the themeselector extension does them but it doesn't break anything.  After changing themes or extensions an **alt+f2 r** enter, may be required (it doesn't hurt anything), and don't panic if after using that combination there is a minor crash and it logs you out, it's harmless and doesn't persist or mean anything is seriously broken.

My biggest problem with Unity, truth be told, is the way it's being shoved down our throats.  I can adapt to just about anything, but why should I have too?  A computer IMO is an extension of the user and as such should feel intuitive, what works for some doesn't work for all.  Historically we've grown up with that "Windows" look & feel, it's what were used to & linux has given us the simple tools to modify it to suit us, that's what I love about it.  Unity (and to some extent Gnome 3.0) is basically an overize Android phone, but Gnome has implemented it better IMO.  Oh well, I'll cut my rambling off there. :)

Only fix - that I immediately know of - is to find the .desktop file (/use/share/apps ?????) and manually edit out the categories you do not want it to appear in ;)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-12
> **philnice said:**
> [QUOTE=cbowman57;10807662]
That sounds interesting. How would i go to wipe both?

sudo apt-get purge unity*
sudo apt-get purge compiz*

then to clean up unused dependencies

sudo apt-get autoremove

> 
**Why do I see all applications twice in the shell?** The  package menu-xdg generates a desktop file from the Debian menu  information, those are in a menu entry that is hidden by default in the  old GNOME menu. Gnome Shell doesn&#8217;t respect those settings and displays  all .desktop files. Remove menu-xdg and you will get a cleaner list of  applications.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-12
> **23dornot23d said:**
> Is there a way to add the Tron Theme Fully - I downloaded it from Deviant Art and its
in a slightly different format I think ....  ?

The Icons and everything are there but I do not know how to set it up ......  [LINK]("http://half-left.deviantart.com/art/GNOME-Shell-Tron-Legacy-191396881")

Uh, kinda.  Copy the icon directory to the folder /usr/share/icons that will let you select them with gnome-tweak-tool.  It looks like the shell theme itself _may_ work but to be read by gnome-tweak-tool or the themeselector it needs a theme.json file and/or a metadata.json, I suppose you could edit one.

A down and dirty method would be to choose an existing shell theme, clone the directory and rename it Tron or something, then replace images one by one from the Tron legacy theme.  Keeping the original name, the DeviantArt theme is probably the one that would be the best starting point.  I've done simple things like changing the corner ripple using this technique.

I'll quit there, It's starting to sound pretty convoluted, I hope you get what I'm trying to explain.  A large part of it you'll just have to figure out as you go, sorry. :)

The key part though is you can replace images item by item, or you can create the .json files that it will need to "read" it via gnome-tweak-tool or the themeselector extension.  I'm sure it's going to take some trial & error to get it right.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
Some people might not want to wait to download an ISO and prefer to create their own gNatty Gnome, especially if they run 64-bit since an ISO isn't really available.  I have also been made aware that some people are having a problem using Ubuquity (the installer in 11.04), their option is to use an [**alternate-installer.iso**]("http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/alternative-download") available from Ubuntu, it uses a text based installer (doesn't mean command line it still walks you through the process with menus).  I can assume if you're looking at this thread you already know what Ubuntu/linux looks and runs like so a "preview" isn't really required.

Anyhow, the recipe/link for the recipe is in post #1 [SIZE="1"](down near the bottom)[/SIZE].

Thanks to everyone involved, and a special thanks to the UGR team & Rico, without their efforts we'd probably be waiting for 11.10 before Gnome 3.0 comes to Ubuntu.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
I don't know if anyone else has experienced this but I've noticed some memory leakage today from gnome-shell, increased by over 100Mb over the course of a few hours.  My down & dirty kludge was to create a launcher.  I named mine scrubber.desktop and placed it in **/usr/share/applications** .  All it does is execute **gnome-shell --replace** which flushes the memory, doing that does not shut anything down or close any other apps you might have open, just a quick flash and you're right back where you started.  Since I use Synapse it's simple & quick, I tried to get it so I could add it to favorites so it would show up in the new panel favorites but I couldn't figure that part out.  Perhaps one of you knows the proper way to do that and you can share it with us.

```

[Desktop Entry]
Icon=gtk-cut
Type=Application
Name=Memory Scubber
X-GNOME-Autostart-enabled=false
Exec=gnome-shell --replace
Name[en_US]=Scrubber
```

After doing some looking around this may only apply to the 64-bit version running Nvidia drivers.  Take a look at System Monitor occasionally to monitor this condition, if it exists on your systems.

The ones that do are the activities, a11y, alt-status (power off), I don't use the dock but I know that one works. I'm not sure if the problems exist because gnome-shell is in a state of flux or it's questionable code in the extensions themselves.  One problem I've noticed is that a lot of them are poorly documented, if they are documented at all & some perform subtle functions that I may not even notice since I'm still learning *how* it's supposed to work. :)

We're breaking new ground so it's a bit of an adventure at this stage.

How does it seem to behave otherwise?

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
> **ivanovnegro said:**
> I have a question guys, what is the difference between your project and the Gnome Remix project, would appreciate some responses.
Sorry, did not have much time to read the whole thread.
Im still searching for the best way to try Gnome 3 on my machine.

The biggest difference is that we actually created a live ISO, but we pulled from their repos, as well as ricotz/testing.  We're also trying to demystify the process & act as a bit of a repository for any & all info we gather pertaining to Gnome 3.0 on Ubuntu. We don't consider this a distro of it's own, it doesn't have the polish for that but it is very functional, though unfortunately it's plagued by the same ATI problems that every linux distro has to overcome. :)

I guess you could consider us something of a virtual linux user group.  Some of the other guys may have a different take on it but that's the way I see it.

BTW, the links to the ISO is in post #1, as well as the recipe to cook your own, which includes a link to UGR.

---

I've given some thought to a v2 but not really sure what changes to make, and how to make them.  I agree that the login bug is a bit of nit, but we're promoting it as being a little "rough" around the edges.  I don't expect that anybody but diehard ubuntu users will even bother looking at it.  I made a very conscious decision when I removed the Windows files from it, I didn't want to get flooded by questions for supporting it after they installed it like a windows app.

Here's the idea I was toying around with yesterday.  Create a user "gnatty" with password "gnatty" on system that is as virgin as possible then run Remastersys in backup mode to create an iso, then using UCK, go into that iso and remove Remastersys and any other residual programs.  That way extensions & themes would be setup for user "gnatty".  Then if they want to install it they can install it, create teir own account with admin privileges then remove user "gnatty".  Copy or rename /home/gnatty so they could transfer the themes & extension setup.  I haven't tried it so I don't know if renaming would work or not.

A sticking point though is going to be the video drivers.  I'm thinking of leaving them out, though I do have a decent installation running the gallium drivers.  It's the ATI folks that present the biggest hurdle.

I'm also curious about the streamlined (I think it's Gnome 3.0 only) version that satanselbow has cooked up.

I'm rambling again. :)

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-13
I found this ..... not sure if it helps ...

> 
**Important note: The livecd username MUST be in lowercase letters only.**
      
If you have issues with livecd user login for the dist mode livecd chances are that there is a problem with either the /etc/passwd or /etc/group files or you have set auto login during install.  These store the users and groups for the system.  If you set autologin in kdm or gdm the live scripts will not be able to create the live user.

              
To fix any issues with the passwd or group files, run the following commands in a terminal window:
      
      
/etc/passwd file fix:
sudo pwck
      
      
/etc/group file fix:
sudo grpck
      
      
After running those and having the issues corrected, try to run remastersys dist again.
      
Please note, backup mode just uses the ones you have on your system in whatever state they are in.
      
The errors come up when there are inconsistencies between /etc/passwd and /etc/group.  This really should never happen unless you have manually deleted entries in either file or somehow had a problem with creating or deleting users.
      
      
[B][COLOR=Red]If it still fails, try the following to find out why.

You can see why it is failing if you add "break=bottom" to the kernel line when booting the livecd.[/COLOR]       [COLOR=Red]

This will show you what is failing and then you can correct it.[/COLOR]       [COLOR=Red]
[/COLOR] [/B]
From This [LINK]("http://www.geekconnection.org/remastersys/info.html")

Not sure if its any help ..... but its the best info I have found up to just .....
thinking about it the login works once the greeter is reset using HTOP .... in the 64 bit one.

So its the  Login greeter >>> I think where the problem lies ...... for the 64 bit version.
(which is easily solved by restarting it)

and

The session one in 32 bit ..... just click it ...... it goes away ...... so no real problem .... just not tidy yet.

### Post by satanselbow on 2011-05-13
Looks like there is plenty a movin' and shaking going here guys :popcorn:

I've not got a lot further - mostly related to the fact that there is some sort of config writeback to the USB-stick (persistence?) by default when testing the remastersys ISOs which renders is useless for subsequent installs hmmm... not got any blank CDs at the mo either (only DVD-R9 disks which are kind of overkill for a 600MB ISO) - a trip to the shops tomorrow for me then.

Sorted out the session list "problem" - just delete any unwanted entries in:

```
gksu nautilus /usr/share/xsessions
```

Check them out with a root gedit first just to check where they are pointing as "Ubuntu Classic (No Effects)" is of course "Gnome Shell (Fallback)" on a technicality and got lost amongst the Ubuntu, User Defined etc etc.

I'm not likely to get a lot further over the weekend as have some family commitments... and a Pagan Picnic (don't ask!).

There really has been some amazing progress here over the last week - good work guys! :popcorn::popcorn::popcorn::popcorn::popcorn:

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
> **23dornot23d said:**
> [MEMORY LEAK]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Memory_leak") ....ok this may be happening on the 64 bit on my computer downstairs
but its hard to tell ..... 


On the 64 bit on my desktop machine - I only have 512M memory on it and it does sometimes go slow .... but have noticed if firefox is going the computer can grind to a halt if I try running anything too intensive with it ..... but not sure how to check or what to look for
to isolate the problem ...... if it is a memory leak ...... or just me running out of it .....

The 32 bit is on my laptop - and not noticed any problems with performance as I have 3 Gig of memory on the Laptop and it runs like a dream. :D

Just pop System Monitor open and look to see what gnome-shell is doing, if it's exceeding 70Mb or so then it's probably leaking.  You can also check how much of your physical and virtual RAM is being used.  If you look and the system is only using 300-400Mb and swapping too you can improve performance by forcing more memory to reside in physical RAM.  I always do this when I set up a system: sudo gedit /etc/sysctl.conf then add at the bottom vm.swappiness=1 I think the default is 60 which is way too conservative and makes your system swap more than it should.

Yeah, I've got 2Gb on my desktop and it's more than adequate for anything I do, 3Gb gives you all kinds of headroom. ;)

What are you using to create the USB stick?  Startup Disk Creator or UNetbootin?  SDC defaults to a small persistence file, but you can toggle it off.  Unetbootin it's off by default, but you can change it.  I've run into that problem myself in the past, I'll boot live, install a vid driver or something and it screws up subsequent boots. :)

Have a good weekend.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
@YesWeCan, you're missing a PPA.  You'll have to go over it again, and for added insurance, go into synaptic > settings > repositories and find where to turn on proposed-updates & backports. ### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
> **cecilpierce said:**
> Couple questions, where did this right hand panel come from and can it be auto-hide ?
I can't unlock the login gizmo's  to set login sound and its set to start up ubuntu and the default start up is gnome-shell.

Hope this makes sense, hang over is still with me a bit...:confused:

Ok, that panel on the right is the dock extension.  Bear with me a second, ok, you must be running the 64-bit version, had to think on that one.  Use nautilus in sudo mode and navigate to /usr/share/gnome-shell/extensions and either move the dock directory out of there, or do what I do and archive it, then delete the directory. You can't make it auto-hide.  You can add xfce4-panel to your autostart and use that, it is very configurable, or you can use AWN, or any other dock that I can think of. 

The login app you have in the photo doesn't work with gdm3.  Haven't messed with login sounds but that may be configurable in system settings under sounds, haven't bothered to look at that yet.

Check those out and let me know what you discover.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
You still don't have the correct PPA's, either that or the VM isn't allowing it to add them properly.  What you're trying to do is something I would never attempt, unless I just wanted to try and break something, but that doesn't mean it isn't possible, just that I've never tried it.

Ok, here goes, assuming it's not a limitation of working in a VM.

```
sudo apt-add-repository ppa:ubuntugnometeam/ppa-gen
sudo apt-add-repository ppa:ubuntugnometeam/gnome3
sudo apt-add-repository ppa:ricotz/testing
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get dist-upgrade
sudo apt-get install ugr-desktop-g3
sudo apt-get upgrade
```

That's pretty much it off the top of my head.  See what that does.

Ok, if that's what the UGR installation instructions have to say that's good.  I just add ricotz for good measure since his stuff is the freshest.  I think UGR merged their repo withe the gnome3-team PPA.

Go for it.

To clarify, I think they merged their ubuntugnometeam/gnome3 ppa with gnome3-team/gnome3

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
> **waspbloke said:**
> Not been following the details on how these Live disks are made so excuse me if this is a stupid question...is it possible to make ATI safe-ish versions?

Yes, as safe as an official Ubuntu disk.  We just won't add any additional proprietary video drivers, which means that probably everyone other than Intel video users will get Gnome fallback by default.  But for an installable disk that's the best way, it will just limit the audience that will actually have gnome shell from a live disk.

It would be a slight departure from our original direction but for people that actually want to have a usable/installable disk with the least amount of problems that's the way to go.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
If you want to shrink it as much as possible install & run bleachbit on it to clean out unused locales, flush the logs, etc..

One thing I did with the UCK build I did was add extra languages, I included French, Spanish, German & Russian.  I figured that covered virtually everybody since it's mostly just us Americans that speak only one language.

I removed libreoffice to save space & a couple other hogs that some people can install from the repos, but it's entirely up to you how you want to go about it.  I think the most important thing is making both versions basically identical for support purposes.

If you are going to pre-install the extensions make sure the application menu & activities extension aren't both active.  Unless it was just a fluke with me last night.  I rather like the App menu, oh, edit it so instead of Applications it reads gNatty. :)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-13
Learned a little bit about extensions tonight, you can check the status and look for errors by hitting the alt+f2 key, type lg, then enter and you'll get a shell of sorts that shows some things relating to extensions.  What I haven't figured out is how to exit from it, I just do the old logout trick.

Anyhow, each extension has a metadata.json file and it has to be constructed to pretty close tolerances.  As an example, my places extension (you guys will probably LOVE to get this one working) is constructed like this 

```
{
**"uuid": "places-menu"**,
"name": "Places Status Indicator",
"description": "Add a systems status menu for quickly navigating places in the system",
"shell-version": [ "3.0.1" ],
"localedir": "/usr/local/share/locale",
"url": "http://git.gnome.org/gnome-shell-extensions"
}
```

Let's look at the uuid info, that line has to match exactly the name of the directory the extension resides in.  For whatever reason this one did not match and therefore would not load.  I simply edited the uuid to match the name of the directory and like magic it started working.

This thumbnail shows it in operation, and for whatever reason it loads Nautilus QUICK!!  Gotta love it.

so now I just have to figure out how to close that debugger or whatever it's called.  If you figure it out let me know please.

Well I feel stupid, the lg stands for Looking Glass and you exit by hitting the Esc key. Doh!  [Here's a link to the Gnome shell cheat sheet]("http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/CheatSheet")

Well excluding Downloads & Music directories it really only takes up about 5.5Gb here so bust out Gparted and carve out 10Gb. :)  

Not sure if it's possible but I think you should be able to bind your existing Downloads and whatever directories you want to save space.  The way I shuffle & replace installations doing that has saved me a lot of grief.  I just bind separate partitions but I think you can bind folders, not positive though.

So it was thought the easy way was to

2 ..... Call in a panel from an existing DE
3 ..... To work out if everything would run together ( just trying to UNIFY things ) 

But since then extensions are being added - tried out ..... themes have been added .... 

Gnome-shell is still in development .... though .... so its a challenge to see what is possible with it. 

ok I have a 8 Gig system now after last nights additions .... kde-full was one of them .... will show that later on

The thing is if you download the DVD that CBowman has put together and just run it as
a live session .... its great ..... as a way of letting people know we are not just stuck

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-14
> **Redsandro said:**
> This is nice. Can you explain a little about what's the deal with this release?

I mean.. is it way tweaked/built/custom and therefore easily breakable when installing/using as steady OS?

Or is it 'just' a nicely compiled liveCD for demonstration purposes with this Gnome3+shell setup also achievable using a few well-chosen commands on an existing 11.04 installation?

I might want to make an image of my setup and try to get something similar working after a dist-upgrade because I am wayyy not in the mood for a fresh installation right now. :P

The gNatty version is 32-bit built from a fresh Ubuntu 11.04 iso, then the PPA's were added and iso customized using UCK.  It's basically what the guys over at Ubuntu Gnome Remix (UGR) are working on. A couple things added, a few removed.  For instance we replaced gdm with lxdm because gdm was problematic trying to do a live session login.  Lxdm complains a bit but it works.  Unless you use ATI video the iso will probably take you straight to a working gnome shell desktop.  The link to the recipe and a couple additional notes, are in post #1 of this thread.  So basically we also provide the information if people want to make their own installation.

---

Yeah, we did just start out sharing what little information about Gnome 3 that we'd accumulated, which as of about 5 days ago wasn't very much.  :lol:

I guess we did discover that we could add a xfce4-panel though. :)

Just looked, we started this thread 4 days ago.  Wow!  We've covered a lot of ground quick.

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-05-14
A question about updates when using gNatty.
Is it ok to run Update Manager form any of the DEs or should one just do this in Gnome Shell?

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-14
It should be safe to run upgrades from any DE.  If you're worried about breaking things after installing you can toggle off the extra PPA's and essentially "freeze"  your installation other than the official ubuntu updates.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-14
> **stepol said:**
> Redsandro , I have it running on a netbook , standard install .
At first I had the same error , then I have uninstalled everything to do with Nvidia , it proposed to uninstall some more packages , just let it do that . Restart and now it runs , not the fastest but it runs .

Hope this helps a little .

Yes, if you guys have the Intel video uninstall nvidia-current, nvidia-common & fglrx. That is likely the culprit.

If you're running it in live mode that may not be an option though.

To get gdm back to default just sudo dpkg-reconfigure gdm.  Kdm is great but it pulls in most of KDE in the process, they coexist but I know not everyone wants KDE on their system.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-14
Once you guys get the basic bugs swatted you'll want to download the gNatty Pack, the link is in post #1.  Just take a look at the README for some additional info.  One thing that is important is to run the gnome-shell-user-theme-extension.deb before activating any extensions related to themes, also create a folder ~/.themes , not sure if that will cause a crash but I think the themeselector expects it to be there even if it's empty (not certain about that but it can't hurt & you will want it later).

Sorry it's not a little more polished but we were expecting only some hardcore Ubuntu users to be trying it out. :)

in the terminal type

choose gdm when it asks you to .....

package ...... as far as I know .......

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-14
> **Redsandro said:**
> Yeah the warning popup freezes on my laptop.

Here some random pics. Redsandro out. :)

Ok, the first pic is normal, just click ok (which you probably did).

The second is the video card conflict most likely, the problem report it's asking about is a minor problem with gnome-session, go into synaptic and make sure the suggested & recommended are selected for install (I think it's desktop-base).

The third pic is just a prompt for a password to set up the gnome-keyring, you can use your login password, or none and choose unsafe storage.

The 4th is that damn gdm thing again, 23dornot23d can probably talk you through that better than I can.

The 5th, the same gnome-session problem again, may be related to the metacity crash.  I haven't seen that one before but it might be cured when you install that desktop-base & sort out the video situation.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-15
> **waspbloke said:**
> I must say, now I've had a chance to just use it for doing my usual stuff, it takes lumps out of Unity. Makes you wonder what the ubuntu team are thinking really, Unity looks and feels like a very poor cousin of gnome-shell.

Using the gNatty pack - in Looking Glass I'm getting a handful of themes reporting errors and not showing up in the selector - "missing theme.json", anybody else? (Dark, Ambiance, iNDENTURE(s) and Elegant)

The ones you mention won't show up in the themeselector, they are metacity themes.  You can use them with gnome-tweak-tool.  It can use the older metacity themes to  draw the titlebar & borders. I may have been wise to have left those out since they may be a distraction but the cat's out of the bag. :)

Good to read that you're exploring things with Looking Glass, I am just now starting to get familiarized with it myself.  Used it to repair those non-working extensions the other night.

Oh yeah, I agree, Unity just doesn't feel the same, though in stock form they look a little similar.

desktop with the Tron theme added ....

1 .... Now have a 1.3 Gig ISO works as near perfect as I can get it for my Nvidia Cards ... and no errors on boot.

2 .... Nine themes are already included as I this as a backup after setting things up using the original


3 ..... The xorg.conf file ...... was thinking of maybe putting 3 or 4 in for people to
easily change if their screen types/sizes were not covered ..... this may be an impossible task as there are so many to cover

4 ..... _*BONUS*_ ...... because Remastersys is already on this disc and configured for use with it ...... 
backups for most USERs upto the size Remastersys allows on one DVD will be simply a case of clicking on Remastersys Backup in the Menu ....... great backup tool ....

( In the past I have never found a one click option we have created one here for small backups )

will now look at the  ( Default.sample in /etc/gdm/PostLogin ) posted

Once this is what we consider to as close to the best experience we can give people
I will run some tests .... on it to see if we can avoid some of the messages popping up.

ok let me know what you think please ....

  from their  home directory in  .local/share/gnome-shell/extensions/

Morning Cecil, yeah Murphy really knows his way around an OS.  Doesn't hurt that he can also write java scripts.  The one that really intrigued me is the one that simulates the session indicator, like the old gnome. :)

I find myself in an odd place, for some traditional reasons I like some of the old features but I really like the way G3 just seems to work almost without thinking about it.  The comfort level I feel with just a couple weeks dedicated use is unreal.  I also like the way new extensions just seem to be showing up without having to get the blessing of Canonical.  I was always more of a keyboard oriented user and between G3, the extensions & Synapse just about anything i want is no more than a few keystrokes and a mouseclick away.  Yesterday I set up Nautilus for single-click and it became even more friendly.  After using it I just can't understand how so many people seem to view it negatively. OTOH, if you have the added distraction of also being in an unfamiliar distro that is a detractor.  I'm glad you decided to pop in and hang with us and seem to be enjoying at least trying it out.  The worst thing that can come of it is you will be able to make an informed decision based on your own experience.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-15
> **philnice said:**
> Niiiiiice....! This way we could understand how things gets done on gnome3 and talk its language. I'm heading to that forum for a while. But i won't miss any of the classes going on here. :)


Yeah, we're getting there. :)

Miss your app indicators?

```

//extension.js
const StatusIconDispatcher = imports.ui.statusIconDispatcher;

function main() {

    // add the notification(s) you want display on the top bar
    // - one per line. Use the english text string displayed when
    // hovering your mouse over the bottom right notification area

        StatusIconDispatcher.STANDARD_TRAY_ICON_IMPLEMENTATIONS['transmission-gtk'] = 'transmission-gtk';
    StatusIconDispatcher.STANDARD_TRAY_ICON_IMPLEMENTATIONS['empathy'] = 'empathy';

}
```

```

//metadata.json
{"shell-version": ["3.0"],
"uuid": "Transmission-gtk",
"name": "Panel Notifications",
"description": "Add application indicators to the panel",
"url": "http://git.gnome.org/gnome-shell-extensions"
}
```

This was a down and dirty piece of code that I borrowed from Murphy's blog to display empathy & transmission to the top panel.

Almost forgot, remember that the uuid has to match the name of the directory the two files that make up the extension reside in.  So these are in ~/.local/share/gnome-shell/extensions/Transmission-gtk

### Post by waspbloke on 2011-05-15
> **23dornot23d said:**
> ok how does it work do you have more details please .......

I'm not claiming any real knowledge about how the kernel works per graphics card driver, but as I understand it, vmlinuz is *the* file that makes the difference. Adding multiple (copy > paste > edit) menu entries to /boot/grub/grub.cfg that point to each of the uniquely named kernel images (generic,ATI,NVIDIA,INTEL) is easy enough. I also remember getting in a pickle some time ago with a kernel with CAPS in the filename, so for caution use 'vmlinuz...ati-generic' etc.

You'll have to see if ATI can handle it or not.

### Post by aeronutt on 2011-05-16
> **cbowman57 said:**
> Run the additional drivers app and see what is activated.  I put both ATI & Nvidia in there so there may be a conflict.

Sorry folks, I should have been more specific.  I've loaded gnatty onto a partition, so I'm booting off of harddisk. I have dual graphics, which doesn't work under regular Ubuntu/Unity either. Heck of a time just getting things to boot on that version also. I have Intel i3 integrated graphics and an AMD Radeon 6630 graphics. None of the AMD proprietary graphics drivers work.  In normal Ubuntu, I finally got the Intel integrated working and the AMD powered off by:

editing rc.local with:
modprobe radeon
echo OFF > /sys/kernel/debug/vgaswitcheroo/switch

and blacklist.conf with:
blacklist radeon

So I'll log back into gnatty and play a bit. But under unity, removing the fglrx driver after an install, never quite worked right IIRC.

Thanks for the help!!

EDIT: Update, unistalled fglrx as described here, and still can't log in using gnome3.
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/FglrxInteferesWithRadeonDriver#Problem:%20%20Need%20to%20fully%20remove%20-fglrx%20and%20reinstall%20-ati%20from%20scratch](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/FglrxInteferesWithRadeonDriver#Problem:%20%20Need%20to%20fully%20remove%20-fglrx%20and%20reinstall%20-ati%20from%20scratch)

Getting the same error after logging in as before:
"XSession arguments unsupported (2); falling back to default session" (or something like that).
And FYI, same thing happens if I try to log in using Unity.

___________________________________

I have now sussed out how to do the torrent properly ....

We now have two more successful 64 bit Distros created .....  just Gnome3 on them.

I decided that it runs so well and now and I have backups on disk USB flash cards and on the USB hard drives  ..... so to go for a full blown system with the other things on that I have previously set up .....

LXDE + Gnome Shell

KDE

Enlightenment

UNITY

and of course

The new Gnome-shell with all the extensions and Themes .....

__________________________________________________________

I know that this is possible because I have already done it ....... the thing is the Distro
is getting bigger ....... was 1.4 Gig on the last burn .....

I am not too bothered about this ....... as its on a Dvd and it could not be reduced
to a CD as far as I could see , then as long as we keep it within the limits of the
Dvd and what the torrent allows me then we are in business.

This does not worry me too much ..... what has worried me is the problems with the
graphics cards .....

___________________________________________________

I do not mind seeing the negative comments some post but if they are just one liners
with no mention of their systems or graphics cards etc ......

**[COLOR=Red]It does not help us ...... to help them ......[/COLOR]** if people get problems tell us specifically what error comes on the screen and what graphics card you use ..... as a start point.

Not it just it wont run on my system .......

We have nowhere to go from that .....

I want to help .......

( When I first started with linux there were very few distros that would pick up the graphics card I had at the time .... now there are very few that do not ...... ) 

I stuck with Nvidia and always have done after finding that out ........

But there is no reason why things cannot change ..... we just need some information
to help ........ something like this ......



[COLOR=Red][B]Gnome Shell does not run on my system because 

I use a xxxxxxx graphics card in a xxxxxxxx computer ........ 

and the way I run it is from live CD or a Install .....

and both fail with this error ...... and here is a screenshot[/B][/COLOR]



Then we might be able to do something about it .......... 

I hope this helps to clarify things

As a response like **[COLOR=Red]my system just does not work[/COLOR]** is just going to get bypassed in future
otherwise it becomes guess work for us and wastes our time guessing at a solution.

The [AMD Radeon 6630]("http://www.google.com/search?client=ubuntu&channel=fs&q=AMD+Radeon+6630&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8#sclient=psy&hl=en&client=ubuntu&hs=uGd&channel=fs&biw=960&bih=585&source=hp&q=ubuntu+AMD+6630+solution&aq=f&aqi=&aql=&oq=&pbx=1&bav=on.2,or.r_gc.r_pw.&fp=9982c75a6c5bd35") graphics ....... <<< click the link

In normal Ubuntu, I finally got the Intel integrated working and the AMD powered off by:

editing rc.local with:

and blacklist.conf with:

So I'll log back into gnatty and play a bit. But under unity, removing the fglrx driver after an install, never quite worked right IIRC.

Thanks for the help!!

EDIT: Update, unistalled fglrx as described here, and still can't log in using gnome3.
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/FglrxInteferesWithRadeonDriver#Problem:%20%20Need%20to%20fully%20remove%20-fglrx%20and%20reinstall%20-ati%20from%20scratch](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/FglrxInteferesWithRadeonDriver#Problem:%20%20Need%20to%20fully%20remove%20-fglrx%20and%20reinstall%20-ati%20from%20scratch)

Getting the same error after logging in as before:
"XSession arguments unsupported (2); falling back to default session" (or something like that).
And FYI, same thing happens if I try to log in using Unity.

Good morning aeronutt, very helpful information, thanks.

It sounds like you're no stranger to finding workarounds on Linux.  Perhaps someone will reply with a working solution.

Since the iso is preloaded with some bleeding edge PPA's that may be the root of this particular problem.  A drastic attempt at a solution might be to install ppa-purge and purge the X-Updates &/or xorg edgers fresh X crack PPA.  That will put the system closer to a standard Natty install and allow you easier access to the standard set of drivers.  Basically revert the sources.list to your installation that works with Unity, retaining the gnome3-team, ubuntugnometeam, & ricotz entries. For troubleshootung & stability you can also remove ricotz without affecting the basic package, what you will be left with is what UGR (Ubuntu Gnome Remix) is using.

If you have the patience & time to continue trying to get G3 shell to work.

### Post by philnice on 2011-05-16
> **aeronutt said:**
> Getting the same error after logging in as before:
"XSession arguments unsupported (2); falling back to default session" (or something like that).
Sorry for bringing this back in, i need to be sure. Now, where you are in fallback mode - obviously - , have you tried to run from terminal, sudo dpkg-reconfigure gdm and select gdm from there?
If so, what happened after the next reboot?
You should probably be able to login normally after that step and, a reboot.

I tried everything I could following that guide and links - no joy. Then came up with my own solution. As root run nautilus. Browse to /var/lib/gdm/.cache/wallpaper replace the wallpapers and rename to exactly the same as the cached items, then reboot - just logout caused my processors to max and some weird stuff with the icon theme changing(?).

Edit: and make sure dimensions of new wp matches those in cache

### Post by aeronutt on 2011-05-17
As to graphics driver, I uninstalled fglrx as it doesn't work with my AMD card.

As to graphics card, I have a dual card laptop with Intel Sandy Bridge i3 integrated graphics, plus an AMD Radeon 6630. This caused a lot of confusion on my initial attemps to use 10.10 or 11.04. On both my standard load of Ubuntu and my other load where I have gnome running, I disabled the AMD graphics and am using the Intel graphics by making the following edits:

To /etc/rc.local, added:
	modprobe radeon
	echo OFF > /sys/kernel/debug/vgaswitcheroo/switch

To /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist.conf, added:
	blacklist radeon

This powers off the AMD graphics and uses the Intel integrated graphics for my other loads. I've made the same edits as above in this gnatty load.

Also for gnatty, I've added 'radeon.modeset=0" during grub boot, and no difference.

Thanks for everyone's help!

### Post by waspbloke on 2011-05-17
> **aeronutt said:**
> I have a dual card laptop with Intel Sandy Bridge i3 integrated graphics, plus an AMD Radeon 6630.

It's not clear if you've tried disabling the integrated card in the BIOS. Seems there are lots of issues with dual card systems and the best starting point is to remove one or disable it so that ubuntu doesn't get all schizophrenic on boot-up. It should then be a matter of working through the issues with the remaining card. From what I know of ATI cards in general, with just the one card presented to the system, you should be able to get your 6630 working well with the open ATI/Radeon driver but you probably will need Mesa 7.11.x rather than the default 7.10.x that Natty comes with.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-17
> **aeronutt said:**
> My BIOS has no option to select graphics! (I've looked several times ;))

Upgrading to Mesa 7.11 is one thing I have NOT tried with vgaswitcheroo and open drivers. I'll give that a try sometime on a fresh load.

All this shouldn't be this difficult.

I agree, if you haven't already, **sudo apt-get purge nvidia-current nvidia-common** and see if that helps.

Something else I thought of, after getting rid of unwanted video drivers, you might even want to add another kernel, totally scrub the one that came installed, your method or ubuntu tweak, to remove all modules, then reinstall one.  That way, if there are any artifacts from the vid drivers, you should have a clean slate sort of speak.

> Is there alot of D/L's of the 64bit ver ?

Oh well, my only concern is that if it doesn't boot up flawlessly it will leave some people with a negative impression regarding G3 shell.  It shouldn't reflect on the shell itself but for a lot of people if the live session or installation doesn't work perfectly it is going to taint their opinion.  I know that in the past I've been turned off by things like that.  A recent example for me is Fedora, I couldn't get a live session, and while it installed ok I absolutely hate their installer, but fortunately there are a multitude of choices.

---

"sudo apt-get --reinstall <name of kernel package>"
or

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-17
Well I just did something stupid, thought I'd broken my shell.  I attempted to drop my Nvidia video from 24 to 16 bit, saved an xorg.conf in the process.  When I rebooted my shell had no top panel and the window frames disappeared (like compiz with no emerald or metacity).  Deleted xorg.conf, everthing came back to normal.  Bottom line, if you run Nvidia and are having problems with the shell it might be due to an old xorg.conf, auto-detect mode appears to be the best.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-17
> **aeronutt said:**
> apt-get purge nvidia-* didn't help.

I'll try reloading a kernel...I've never done that before so it'll be a learning experience. :)  How does one go about 'totally scrubbing' the current kernel? I have used the kernel cleaner in Ubuntu-Tweak, but the current kernel doesn't show up there.

I'll google also.

EDIT:
So...something like this? "sudo apt-get --reinstall <name of kernel package>"

I just search one out using synaptic, I'll attach a screenshot showing the 3 important packages.  Ubuntu tweak has a safeguard, it will only delete a kernel that you are not using.  If using ubuntu tweak, delete the kernel, then use the clean config button.

BTW, the kernel in synaptic that I'm using has a bug so I'm going to revert to a previous version, but it does work so if you have to use it temporarily that will be fine.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-17
> **cecilpierce said:**
> I have xorg.conf and no problems, what wud happen if I took it out?
AND got new suff on desktop I didnt have last night, also how many themes are in here now? I only have 9
Dunno Cecil, I've pared mine down to 6 shell themes that show up using the theme selector extension.

   Using gnome-tweak-tool I show 13 GTK+ themes.

I don't use Nautilus to draw my desktop but I really like the clean look & wallpaper you've chosen for your setup.

Oh, the xorg.conf thing, not sure if that would cause a problem with your system or not, depending on driver version, some won't work without one.  If you delete it and then nvidia-settings says  you're not running a Nvidia driver all you have to do is go to a terminal and do a **sudo nvdia-xconfig** and a new one will be created.  Reboot and everything should be right with the world again. :)

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-17
> 
all I'm interested in at the moment is the user profile with G3 and all the fancy stuff by default
____________________     ________________ [U][I][B]This bit gets useruser to login .....

I think that you are the only person with a copy of this ..... other than the site I sent it through ....
its purely for testing and then if we can sort some things out over the net maybe then we
can put a single version out ...........

If you would please /// try this ......

ok with **useruser** ....... to get it going we need to do as below I just tried it on mine and it works this way

Try this if you would please ......

_______________________________

Change the desktop environment to lxde

login as

keith >>> [COLOR=Red]administrator
[/COLOR] 
goto menu - administration ....

**users and groups**


using the password  >>> [B]administrator 
[/B] 
set the useruser  .......  to [COLOR=Red]**autologin **
[/COLOR] 


next press ctrl+alt+f1

login to keith in this terminal as keith

password
[B]administrator
[/B] 
then type gdm


gdm login will startup a seperate login screen ..........

now you can now click on **useruser** and get in from gdm


Hope it works ok ..... 

I am pretty confident it will .....

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-17
I do not know a lot about networks - and you probably know loads
with running BB's ...... but I guess there is no harm in finding some
more information ..... 

Maybe someone else here will spot the problem better than I can 

**sudo lshw -C network**

**ifconfig eth0**


But if someone can help you to get it going and find out why it is no going that
would be a bonus - as then we can add it if its just a missing package ........

___________________________________________________________________

If you run the commands I gave earlier there is no need for LAN to see if the new 
GS Tron Style desktop loads in ok ......

Just following the instructions in this previous link ........ 

I have tried to make it more legible as it was confusing before ....

> 

**1......  FROM THE MAIN LOGIN SCREEN**
Change the desktop environment to LXDE ..... (you did this ok)

2 ...... **LOGIN AS**
keith >>> [COLOR=Red]administrator [COLOR=Black](you did this ok)
[/COLOR][/COLOR] 

3 ...... **USERS AND GROUPS**
goto menu - administration ....**  Users and Groups**
You have to enter the password  >>> [B]administrator 
[/B] set the useruser  .......  to [COLOR=Red]**autologin **
[/COLOR] 
4 ....... Next press    **ctrl+alt+f1**
login to keith in this terminal as keith
password[B] = administrator
[/B] then at the $ enter

**gdm**


5 ...... **The gdm login screen will now appear**
now you can now click on **useruser** it autologs in now .......


[B]I think this should at least get to see if the themes are there .... and that
the desktop works ok ......


Then we can continue and solve why the LAN is not loading up ........


[]("http://img811.imageshack.us/img811/7808/0latest.png")

[/B]

### Post by philnice on 2011-05-18
> **millworm said:**
> Hi everyone,

Thanks allot for this! However i'm not able to use this?

I tried to install this from my laptop, but whenever i wan't to install or try ubuntu  i get error messages stating that GNOME3 failed to load (prop. due driver issues) and a system problem has been detected?

Got ATI Radeon graphic card. Any way i can circumvent this?
You should ignore that message, choose "other" from the drop down list on login, input your credentials, then the message, "xsession unsupported number of arguments..." and you're in fallback mode since gnome3 - shell couldn't started at the moment. From there, you could try to enable the open source (ati) driver which would work best with gnome 3, but since you're on a live system i can't guarantee that your actions would have an effect.
All these messages, could be ignored and by-passed after installation. I know, i have tried my self.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-18
> **millworm said:**
> I'm indeed trying out the 32 bit version.

The first error message i get is that it can't find startx or something. Also the GDM is broken and i can't select anything. I just logins into the classic ubuntu desktop. That's when i get the error messages in my previous post.

Ok, that's normal, it will default to gnome shell but if your vidcard doesn't have the correct drivers, or support the fglrx drivers that were installed on that iso it will default to fallback mode.  Unfortunately the live session just isn't going to work for you, but after installation you can probably get it running, and there are people here that have been having luck with ATI cards.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-05-18
> **mainstream said:**
> I can't choose anything in the drop-down list or login, i only see the login screen for a split second. I automatically get the desktop environment. 

THanks for your help anyways :)

If you're getting a desktop environment that's all you need to do an installation.  If you already have a working 11.04 installation then getting this to run gnome shell shouldn't be a problem.  There are just special problems associated with ATI cards and their drivers, depending on the card you may or may not have luck with the fglrx drivers.  You may have to use open source or the Radeon driver.

But yeah, for the 32-bit there is no user or pw information needed.  Don't give up yet. :)

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-19

Water was pretty choppy, make for a nice picture, but the beach wasn't very inviting.  Not a good day for bikini watching. :)

Cheers Charles ,

Its been downloaded 6 times now so things may pick up a little .....

Here its mainly countryside .... just got back ..... fields fields and more fields.
Except for one brilliant picturesque canal 300 kms long the Nantes to Brest canal
I have biked about 40 km of it in the past .... that is on separate occasions going out 20 km from where I live ( no bikini's around here .... ) ok hope you get the file ok and it
works a lot better this time ....... if not then we can start discussing  why not .....
and move forward with the problems hopefully ..... as there is one problem that stops me using the panel fully at the moment ....... in a panel js file ......once thats sorted I can add all of the newer panel features to it  ......

Be careful how big the code becomes seems to be quite a drain on the system at load up time ......

I did something else too ..... to reduce the size a little a few packages have gone but can be installed from the software center or synaptic ......

Mono and Tomboy Banshee ...... are not on at the moment ..... while testing .....
( will keep the Distro to about 1.5 Gig ..... and try not to go any higher now ....

These can always be added later as they are pretty well used and stable packages.

If anybody else thinks things can be removed to get this down to CD size tell me and I will try ....... the extra desktops and users were not as big a overhead as I expected
as they are all very clean and KDE is just used with cairo-dock

But if you want the full thing ..... do these when you get it ......
you always have to do update to rebuild the listings for it to work ....

sudo aptitude update
sudo aptitude install kde-full

you can also do 

sudo aptitude safe-upgrade 

If you want to see if the other packages break the system
and by running it from Dvd its a good way to find out before doing it to your main system.

As everything will be running in memory only ..... and disappear on shutdown.
Really do hope people like the way this is set up and works now ....
If not then its back to the drawing board ....

By the way this is being typed while using it and the download of a torrent was also done on here
so the network works on mine .... 
I just hope it works for you all now .... oh I added a weather ICON too .... in Cairo-dock let me know if it works
ok now too ...... 

  [**TODAYS SCREENSHOTS -  1**]("http://img594.imageshack.us/img594/4933/screenshot8rt.png")

  [**GIMP RUNNING - 2**]("http://img851.imageshack.us/img851/7605/screenshot9vx.png") ( and in LXDE with Gnome shell ..... just do Alt+f2  ..... gnome-shell --replace )

I added adblock plus and No scripts to the firefox too .... these may still need you to set them up
for your trusted sites.

Hi 23dornot23d, maybe you know something about creating and saving activities
in gnome3 ? Is this possible in some way?

As seen in Natty's Kubuntu there are some kind of special activity-collections, which 
you can load up and then do your work with a 'predefined application-environment'.

own 'working-activity-group'.

your working places, but I like the idea of having this feature for predefining
any wanted application-group and call it by its name fe:'1' as 'activity-popup'.
