### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-15
Well guys, really pleased with this one.  I think I've covered all the bases and this will likely be the final version, barring some special situation.  A 32-bit version will follow within a week if everything goes well.


[http://linuxtracker.org/index.php?page=torrent-details&id=603bd567f7006e0835c2d8e095d390dbc8b1dfef](http://linuxtracker.org/index.php?page=torrent-details&id=603bd567f7006e0835c2d8e095d390dbc8b1dfef)

As usual, it's going to be slow creeping into the web, please keep it seeded as much as possible.

**Installation Note**

Thank you for trying gNatty Gnome amd64 v3 Live!

Before you begin read this document thoroughly.

This is a respin of Ubuntu 11.04 amd64 desktop with Gnome 3.0 shell installed.

- It was created with the recipe provided by the UGR (Ubuntu Gnome Remix) team ([http://ugr.teampr0xy.net/](http://ugr.teampr0xy.net/)) for UGR 0.1.1 "Nebula" and some packages from the ricotz/testing ppa.

- It is recommended that you do not try to upgrade and existing installation, I cannot predict the results.

- Known bugs, will not install from the grub menu, must be installed from the desktop environment.  In Gnome classic it can be found by clicking on System > Administration > Install Ubuntu 11.04 release.  There seems to be a problem with Nvidia 173 drivers, remove those and upgrade to the later version.

- If for some reason you find yourself logged out you are "Live session user", there is no password.

- Do not choose automatic login as an option while you are installing, you can work around it but it might make things more difficult.

- After installing you will want to go to the Gnome classic desktop & update your video driver.  For those of you that run Intel or Nvidia video cards there should be no problems.  For those having problems with ATI & Ubuntu 11.04, that's just an inherent problem with the current Ubuntu release.

- Once your video driver is installed you should be able to reboot and choose "GNOME" when you log back in.

- After the shell itself is functioning you can then add extensions & themes.  

- Place gNatty Themes.tar.gz in your Home directory and extract, the contents should end up in ~/.themes.

- Place gNatty Icons.tar.gz in ~/.local/share & extract, the contents should end up in ~/.local/share/icons.

- Place gNatty Extensions.tar.gz in ~/.local/share/gnome-shell, the contents should end up in ~/.local/share/gnome-shell/extensions.

- I have included a screenshot of my desktop after using this installation iso.

- Also included is the wallpaper I use, I find it looks very nice with the Atolm theme.

Full credit is given to Canonical, the Gnome team, UGR, Finn Murphy and the assortment of artists & authors that have contributed to this little project.

If you run into a problem, or just want to say that everything went well you can find me in this thread on Ubuntu forums [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1754198](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1754198) and I can be found throughout the forum as cbowman57.

If you spend time on Facebook I also visit the UGR page at [https://www.facebook.com/pages/UGR/120931067991846](https://www.facebook.com/pages/UGR/120931067991846) as Charles Bowman.

There is no warranty expressed or implied. I am making this available with the assumption that whoever gives it a try already has some working knowledge of Linux and/or Ubuntu.

Now read it again before you begin.  Good luck & have fun.

---

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-06-16
**gajim** is for instant messaging .... [LINK]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gajim")

Have a good bike ride too - I was out on mine yesterday .... was my Birthday so a nice meal
down by the side of the canal with an Old Friend .... it rained a little but still turned out to be a good day ......

I am presently trying to cut the size down of the ISO I made to add other things to it ..... seems if we have
DVD's and REMASTERSYS that allow for 4 Gig .... and most Flash USB drives are now being made bigger 
I think we change with the times ..... like how many 1.44mb restrictions stopped developers moving on
technology will change ...... one day 4 Gig will seem like the 1.44mb disks do to use now.

So my intention is to get the best software on a disk and the best desktop environment .... yet still to get it
running on reasonably old hardware too ..... as my old desktop machine here has only 512 Meg of Ram and
a 6200 Nvidia Card in it ..... got UNITY , COMPIZ , GNOME-SHELL all running plus a decent screensaver -
It was interesting that Fedora 15 would not allow Gnome-Shell to install in under 640Meg of Ram on that same machine  ..... not sure why yet though as it runs in 470 Meg on my remake with Firefox running and conky and cairo-dock .... 

People can always burn disks for their friends too now that Remastersys works on this disk with K3b for
burning the Dvd too ......

Will look at the panel later ..... as I am still using Cairo-Dock at present instead as it has everything I need.

But I do like options ..... ;)

### Post by shuttleworthwannabe on 2011-06-17
Hi cbowman57, does v3 allow wubi install? I am not partitioned at the moment, and am using ubuntu through wubi; I am keen to test your g3 shell rendition so I am asking.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-17
> **shuttleworthwannabe said:**
> Hi cbowman57, does v3 allow wubi install? I am not partitioned at the moment, and am using ubuntu through wubi; I am keen to test your g3 shell rendition so I am asking.
Thanks
swb

No, I'm sorry, it does not.  I removed that support because it creates too many problems and makes troubleshooting a PITA.  Running linux as if it were an app under windows just runs contrary to my philosophy.  With the available drive space people have these days I feel it is just too simple to use GParted (included on the live image) to carve out a 10-20Gb partition and do an actual install to try it out.  If you worry about grub messing up your windows installation grub can be installed to a usb flash drive with good results.

I should have probably included that info in the installation notes but I appreciate you asking the question.  I hope that you will reconsider the installation options & give it a go.

I multi-boot myself & keep a Windows 7 partition for those times when nothing else will do.  I certainly don't believe in limiting a person's choice when it comes to computing tools.

Another option is to use the UGR recipe to try and upgrade your existing wubi installation, in addition to their instructions I add ppa:ricotz/testing, which as of a couple days ago was required for installing extension & theme support.  Do not install the extensions from the ppa though, you can then download the gNatty Pack and install the themes & extensions per the included installation notes.  If you go that route let me know how it goes, I'm curious & it may result in leaving wubi in future versions if it runs trouble free for you.

(I realize you know what you're doing in Ubuntu but wanted to spell things out clearly for other less experienced users)

I understand the work laptop thing. :)

Jockey-gtk is included, ubuntu-restricted-extras were added.  No, the flash plug-in is what comes with the restricted-extras.  I wanted to adhere to the generic Natty as much as possible, though I personally usually add the Adobe flash for 64-bit via ubuntu-tweak, which I believe I left out.  Synapse was added because I can't do without it.

It is a live DVD, but I'm sure you're already familiar with the way the stock video drivers affect Unity & Gnome shell.  If you can burn it to usb flash drive with persistence file though you should be able to customize it some.  If your laptop has Intel video though it may work as-is, that's something I haven't personally tested.

I'm very much interested i how you think it stacks up against Fedora.  My previous experience with Gnome 3 was on OpenSuse & I think this compares favorably.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-18
Thank you bulldog, thought I'd altered kovtray that went into the package but apparently I picked it from the wrong partition.  Very simple matter to adjust icon size in the kovtray extension.js

Adjust the number that is in bold.

```
function main(metadata) {
        MessageTray.MessageTray.prototype._setExpandedSummaryItem = function (summaryItem) { };
        MessageTray.Source.prototype.ICON_SIZE = **24**;
}
```

24 looks nice on my system but you might want to experiment with different sizes.

I'm no java script programmer but I've found that it's pretty easy to make minor changes to the extensions & even the shell itself with a little poking around.

I am extremely happy with this version & glad to get some feedback on how it works for other people. I think the approach I took with the themes & extensions is in line with some of the suggestions received and discussions we've had.  Going to try and work on the 32-bit version next week, a couple days later than I'd planned.  Think I'll call it v3.1 since I didn't make notes, so while I want an exact duplicate of this one, I'm certain to have a minor change or two. :)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-18
[LEFT]Finn quietly deposited a really cool extension the other day in his [repository _called statusbuttoncss._]("http://www.fpmurphy.com/gnome-shell-extensions/")

It's primary function was to reduce the icon padding of the status icons & make them more closely approximate the spacing of the icons displayed by the eureka extension (at least I think that was his intent since he's been silent on the subject).  I found that I could improve the aesthetics even further with a little editing.

```
this._iconActor = new St.Icon({ icon_name: iconName,
                                        icon_type: St.IconType.**FULLCOLOR**,
                    **icon_size: Main.panel.button.get_child().height,**
```By changing the text from SYMBOLIC to FULLCOLOR they can be made to use your icon theme. By adding the text in line 3 they inherit the size of your other panel icons.  I think it's a really nice extension & hope some of you might find a use for it also.

While I feel that most of these extensions add a lot of gnome 2 functionality I think they also enhance gnome 3.0 and show how configurable it is.

I've added an image showing the result of using this extension with the modifications I've described.

BTW, to remove my username from the status menu I comment out the lines as shown here in statusMenu.js

```

    _updateUserName: function() {
      //  if (this._user.is_loaded)
      //    this._name.set_text(this._user.get_real_name());
      //  else
```

(Currently lines 108-110)

[/LEFT]

I'd been searching for 'autohide' for the top bar, and found a few who seemed to have been successful in other distros. Here's a few examples:

### Post by shuttleworthwannabe on 2011-06-21
I did it!

(1) Live DVD x64 v3
(2) Hardware has nvidia gforce 330m
(3) Boot up with Live DVD
(4) After ISOLINUX....press F6 and select NOMODSET (?spelling)
(5) Let is load into desktop and select Gnome Classic (no effects)
(6) Install as usual
(7) Reboot and login into Gnome Classic
(8) Use jockey-gtk to install nvidia drivers
(9) Reboot
(10) Login into GNOME session and voila, gnatty in its full glory!

Now:
(1) How to get the GUI windows to look and honor the general theme--they are too sharp and lack lusture
(2) gnome-tweak-tool is handy and I have managed to setup fonts
(3) I use chromium-browser, why are the close/minimize/max buttons on the left side? How to make it look at Gnome 3 theme?

This is exciting...

---

The difference between UGR and this should be clarified by the maker of this release,butI can tell you what I know and if I'm mistaken,cbowman can correct me.

The UGR team gives you the tools to install Gnome-Shell on a fresh Natty install.
In simplified terms,cbowman made a copy of his install with UGR,after modifying extensions and themes and testing of course.
This copy is made available for you to install Gnome-Shell on a easy manner.

So if you do a clean Natty install,and use the UGR ppa and the gnome3 ppa ,you should basically get the same,but without the extra's which cbowman has build in.

### Post by bulldog on 2011-06-21
> **millworm said:**

 This would be the 32 bit release,this should have the new network manager also.
What did you do to get it not working?
Did you enable the ricotz ppa?
If you did enable the ricotz ppa,it would be advised to disable it again,it can and will break your install and it's not intended for novice users,only if you know what you're doing and can come to solutions,you can enable this one.

If it did work before you can go back to a previous version of the network manager with synaptic.
Find the network-manager in synaptic with the search,select it,and go to Package in your menu.
Select force version and select the previous one which worked.

My bad,I did re read your message and I understand you have no more internet on that computer.
The same happened to me.
I had no internet,not much time at hand,so I did a fresh install over it,should be no more then twenty minutes or so,and you're up and running again.
I don't think there's a .deb or something like that available for the network manager,may be a tar.gz on launchpad or the site for network manager,but I'm not sure at all,and I don't know if it will work.

### Post by bulldog on 2011-06-21
> **shuttleworthwannabe said:**
> How do I install new themes? Drag and drop? but where?

New themes should be extracted to ~/.themes for the user.
BUT this will only work for the user NOT root.
You'll see synaptic is not following your chosen theme.
To get that working you'll need to copy the themes which are in your ~/.themes folder,to /usr/share/themes,this should be done with sudo.
You could open nautilus with ALT-F2 gksu nautilus and copy the themes from ~/.themes to /usr/share/themes.

If done root will have the same theme as you are using as a user.
In fact you don't need the ~/.themes folder any more,but keep it there to extract and explore new themes and if you like and use them,copy them over.

The tar.gz which came with the ISO,for extensions and themes,read the install notes from cbowman which is in the same folder as the tar.gz are.

### Post by bulldog on 2011-06-21
> **millworm said:**
> There was something with an ugr update. I noticted to late it removed the network manager.

Yes I had UGR updates too,but nothing removed my network-manager,I have to say I'm on the v3 but this shouldn't matter.
I have a Laptop with the same version as you have,will check what happens when I update.
I'll 'be back in 10 minutes.

Yep you're right,this happens with a partial upgrade which removes your UGR desktop and replace it with the ubuntu-desktop.
Also your NEW network-manager will be replaced with the OLD one,which obvious doesn't work.
I did not do the upgrade thanks to you,I know what would happen when I did.

But the only advice I can give you is to re-install on top of it and REFUSE the partial upgrade,or wait till cbowman release the NEW 32bit version one of these days.
Or,if your machine is capable,download the x64 v3 and try that one,either way,I don't think replacing the network manager will get you to a working gnome-shell again,because you have installed the ubuntu-desktop and removed the UGR-desktop.

### Post by bulldog on 2011-06-21
Strange,the 'update' is only for 32bit version,just checked,and no partial upgrade for the x64.
But what not is can come tomorrow!

BE CAREFUL WITH PARTIAL UPGRADES,LOOK VERY CAREFULLY WHAT IT WILL DO,IT WILL REMOVE UGR-DESKTOP AND REPLACE IT WITH THE UBUNTU-DESKTOP,IN THE SAME MOVE,IT WILL REMOVE YOUR NETWORK-MANAGER AND WILL TRY TO REPLACE IT WITH THE OLD ONE,WHICH COULD FAIL!!

BE WARNED!!

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-21
> **Roasted said:**
> Can someone clue me in on what the difference is from this and UGR? Just trying to grasp the concept of what's going on in here. Thanks. :)

Also, is there a 32 bit version? Or does anybody know if Intel Atoms support 64 bit?

Hey Roasted, very little is different from UGR, it uses the 2 ppa's they do but to v3 I added ricotz/testing.  As far as I know UGR doesn't have an iso yet, nor do they try to package themes & extensions virtually guaranteed to work with with each other for Ubuntu.

So what I attempted to do was give people an iso they could burn & a collection of themes & extensions that should work without having to search the internet.  So basically it just provides for an easy way to try Gnome 3.0 shell on Ubuntu and have some extra bells & whistles to make it interesting & get them started.

cbowman57,

Thanks this is such a breath of fresh air for Ubuntu! Well done. It is fast, responsive and Adobe Flash player works without a glitch (Ubuntu Natty 11.04 x64 the flash is choppy--and I know there is a fix for this somewhere).

My current challenges:
1. mouse cursor does _not_ want to change to desired cursor--this is a [bug]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/compiz/+bug/786325") in ubuntu; command line ```
sudo update-alternatives --config x-cursor-theme
``` also does not stick--I have a schizophrenic mouse cursor--changes type and color on each screen and when hovering over a hyperlink. Annoying...
2. Theme changing in Gnome--currently has to be done by copying the themes into the personal .themes folder; can be discouraging; will wait for a better simple and GUI based theme changer.

Otherwise, well done--feel very much like F15, but with the human touch of Ubuntu.

---

### Post by millworm on 2011-06-22

Yep you're right,this happens with a partial upgrade which removes your UGR desktop and replace it with the ubuntu-desktop.
Also your NEW network-manager will be replaced with the OLD one,which obvious doesn't work.
I did not do the upgrade thanks to you,I know what would happen when I did.
 
But the only advice I can give you is to re-install on top of it and REFUSE the partial upgrade,or wait till cbowman release the NEW 32bit version one of these days.
Or,if your machine is capable,download the x64 v3 and try that one,either way,I don't think replacing the network manager will get you to a working gnome-shell again,because you have installed the ubuntu-desktop and removed the UGR-desktop.
 
Thanks for the reply. However i didn't ubuntu-desktop or another network manager for me. It just upgraded something with ugr-desktop. With that upgrade it removed gnome-network-manager and something else. I hope a new update comes soon.

Or does someone have a ETA on the new release? Hehe. THanks

Putting the final touches on gNatty i686 v3.1 right now.  After that's completed it will just be a test boot & install to verify it, then should start seeding within 4 hours of this post. ### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-22
Post #1 has been updated, rearranged a bit, and the link for gNatty Gnome i686 v3.1 Live added.

If you already tried the 64-bit v3 this will feel immediately familiar, everything is virtually the same. There was one omission though, I forgot to install numlockx, so if you like having your numeric keypad on by default you might want to install that.

Gdm was downgraded & locked, you can either upgrade to the newer version the first time you do an upgrade, or using aptitude you can lock it by using **sudo aptitude hold gdm** as well as locking it in synaptic.  I then suggest you use sudo aptitude safe-upgrade in the future so that it won't be overwritten.  If there is a way to pin apps with apt-get I would appreciate someone telling me how to do it that way also.  (Applies to v3 & v3.1)

---

### Post by silkworm2.5 on 2011-06-22

I tried installing gNatty 64 bit v3.0 onto my USB drive, but it fails to boot. It simply skips the USB drive, and proceeds directly to my GRUB screen.
I have tried Unetbootin, Startup disk creator, and the pendrivelinux live USB creator. None work. My BIOS is definitely set up properly,

Can anyone help please?

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-22
> **silkworm2.5 said:**
> Hi. I was wondering if you could help me please?

I tried installing gNatty 64 bit v3.0 onto my USB drive, but it fails to boot. It simply skips the USB drive, and proceeds directly to my GRUB screen.
I have tried Unetbootin, Startup disk creator, and the pendrivelinux live USB creator. None work. My BIOS is definitely set up properly,

Can anyone help please?

So, when your system boots up it gives the option to boot from USB, you select USB and it goes straight to your grub menu?

If that's the case it sounds like the iso is corrupted & doesn't match the md5sum. 

 Now if it boots the system and just ignores your USB drive then it's something to do with your system (hardware).  Can you hold the f9 key or something when it starts up to get a boot menu from bios?

---

### Post by silkworm2.5 on 2011-06-22
When I hold F9 it displays a list of connected drives to boot from. the USB drive is listed, and has recently booted Fedora.
GTKhash shows that the Checksum is the same as the one included.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-22
That is a puzzle, I always use Startup Disk Creator to burn my installation to test from & it works brilliantly.  I use UNetbootin for the occasional stubborn iso's.

How about using GParted (or your favorite utility) to delete the partition & recreate it, just to be certain, then try it again.  If the numbers match there shouldn't be any problem installing or booting from USB.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-22
I had some weirdness like that a couple weeks ago, first time I actually took my machine to a shop.  He had to clean the crap out of it and I went ahead and replaced the MB & CPU anyway.  He said there was crud caked on the back of the MB, for all I know it worked fine after he cleaned it up.

If I had it to do over again I would have just replaced the MB myself and upgraded but I was in a hurry and I was trying to throw him some business.

Oh, in my case, I think the gunk on the back of the board was soaking up our humid air and shorting across the IDE pins or something since that was the code that his hardware diagnostic tool kept pulling up, and the error kept popping up even with all the drives turned off in Bios.  I booted from USB and it still kept crashing so I don't know if it was just a failing MB, even though it was less than a year old, or caused by the damp crud.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-06-24
> 
Hi there--it took me a while to get the full DVD sized ISO  downloaded-something like 2 nights (I let it run over 2 nights; stopped  during the day).
Glad to see that you got it alright .....

There are a few seeds now and its odd with these torrents - sometimes they seem to pick up and run
perfectly from the Linuxtracker site and other times the seeded and peers end up in brackets to the
side .......... 

( seems to be when this happens for whatever reason that the torrent does not run as well )  
( I did ask the question on the site but as of yet have had no reply as to why it does it )
( but I am just happy that they run the service - as it does seem to be one of the easier ones to use )

> 

Does anyone else get tired of reading through the forum and seeing  people that should know better tell others that Gnome 3.0 shell &  Unity can't coexist within the same installation?

[ unity ( which is a plugin for compiz )] 
[ gnome-shell ..... which runs on MUTTER ]

The thing that they both have in common is that they both run on is the same kernel.

--------------------------------------------------

This is one thing that does annoy me - as many times now I have posted videos where I
show that I can swap quite happily between the two systems .....  :D
and as you and I know the two systems will run quite happily together.

I have launchers on the desktop for unity --replace and compiz --replace 
( and of course to get back gnome-shell --replace ) switch when I want from system to system
and its got better as time as gone on too ...... much faster for me now .....

As the wording implies - replace ...... when we run the commands what happens is the systems are replaced and should not interfere with each other if the developers have written the code for one - it should not affect the other.

If for instance someone has a case where it does affect the other - then it should be highlighted and we will track it down as a problem - up to this moment in time I have [COLOR=Red]**NOT**[/COLOR] seen one ........

> 
Beginning to wonder if I'm the exception and I somehow managed to have just **exactly** the perfect mix of hardware or something that hides all the bugs from me. :smile:
Well thats 2 of us ..... 


> 
How "buggy" do you think Gnome 3.0 is at this time?  I've been reading  lots of comments to that effect & I'm just not experiencing them.


( It did concern me the time you mentioned the Network manager and I did see this on a friends computer - wireless showing up the NETWORK but not picking it up to use it - that was the only time I noticed a big problem and that I would hope should be fixed now - other than that extensions and themes being put in the wrong places sometimes with wrong privileges can cause problems but should not be show stoppers as in the past - removing them and replacing them soon sorts things out.)

If something annoys me ..... 
I soon bring it to the forefront as [COLOR=Red]**I dislike errors immensely and want them sorting properly**[/COLOR] .....
problems have been solved and its happened all the way through the process here and we can see it ..... 

That is more important to me ...... people are here to help ..... and that they try their hardest to do so ......

__________________________________________________

I am using gnome-shell for my Production machine and have been for a while now.

( I would not be doing that if it was buggy )

### Post by Roasted on 2011-06-24
> **cbowman57 said:**
> Does anyone else get tired of reading through the forum and seeing people that should know better tell others that Gnome 3.0 shell & Unity can't coexist within the same installation?

How "buggy" do you think Gnome 3.0 is at this time?  I've been reading lots of comments to that effect & I'm just not experiencing them.

Beginning to wonder if I'm the exception and I somehow managed to have just **exactly** the perfect mix of hardware or something that hides all the bugs from me. :)

Unity is Gnome 2.X based. Shell is Gnome 3.0 based. That's the only reason they clash, because once you bring Gnome 3.0 up to speed, Unity tanks. Ubuntu 11.10 will bring a different experience, when Unity is based on Gnome 3.0. They should be able to co-exist then.

---

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-06-24
> 
Unity is Gnome 2.X based. Shell is Gnome 3.0 based. That's the only  reason they clash, because once you bring Gnome 3.0 up to speed, Unity  tanks. Ubuntu 11.10 will bring a different experience, 
The fact UNITY cannot operate in Gnome 3.0 is a UNITY problem .... not a gnome-shell one.

> 
when Unity is  based on Gnome 3.0. They [COLOR=Red]**should**[/COLOR] be able to co-exist then.
This of course is true .... and gnome-shell can quite easily drop back to Gnome 2.32 mode where both can live happy together ......

and if you go into [*11.10 oneiric ..... you can see how much effort they are putting in to make sure that UNITY and GNOME_SHELL will still work happily together *]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1789394")..... and what they think of the GNOME3 integration.


The new Lightdm ..... not sure if there is a option for Gnome-shell built into it yet ......
The global menu system ...... not sure that there is a switch being made to easily swap it on or off  .....

I didn't think it possible but Transmission allowed me to keep both the openbittorrent & linuxtracker torrents so I can seed both simultaneously.

I've been corresponding with Carl over at linuxtracker trying to figure out what happened and how to avoid the confusion in the future.  I really appreciated him sending me email and saying, "hey, you gonna seed this or not, people are waiting" (words to that effect).  Without that people would have become frustrated and it would have reflected on this spin.

Thanks guys.

Ok, just want the torrent downloaded.  I know with Transmission you can point it to the directory already downloaded and it will verify and allow you to seed it.  You shouldn't have to download the whole package again. :)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-24
> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
[ unity ( which is a plugin for compiz )] 
[ gnome-shell ..... which runs on MUTTER ]

The thing that they both have in common is that they both run on is the same kernel.

--------------------------------------------------

This is one thing that does annoy me - as many times now I have posted videos where I
show that I can swap quite happily between the two systems .....  :D
and as you and I know the two systems will run quite happily together.

I have launchers on the desktop for unity --replace and compiz --replace 
( and of course to get back gnome-shell --replace ) switch when I want from system to system
and its got better as time as gone on too ...... much faster for me now .....

As the wording implies - replace ...... when we run the commands what happens is the systems are replaced and should not interfere with each other if the developers have written the code for one - it should not affect the other.

If for instance someone has a case where it does affect the other - then it should be highlighted and we will track it down as a problem - up to this moment in time I have [COLOR=Red]**NOT**[/COLOR] seen one ........

Well thats 2 of us ..... 




( It did concern me the time you mentioned the Network manager and I did see this on a friends computer - wireless showing up the NETWORK but not picking it up to use it - that was the only time I noticed a big problem and that I would hope should be fixed now - other than that extensions and themes being put in the wrong places sometimes with wrong privileges can cause problems but should not be show stoppers as in the past - removing them and replacing them soon sorts things out.)

If something annoys me ..... 
I soon bring it to the forefront as [COLOR=Red]**I dislike errors immensely and want them sorting properly**[/COLOR] .....
problems have been solved and its happened all the way through the process here and we can see it ..... 

That is more important to me ...... people are here to help ..... and that they try their hardest to do so ......

__________________________________________________

I am using gnome-shell for my Production machine and have been for a while now.

( I would not be doing that if it was buggy )

Ok, well I try to respond when & where I can to dispel some of the myths.

I never looked under the hood to see what you put in the launcher to switch from DE to DE, that's pretty neat. :)

That network manager problem was shortlived as I remember & was cured simply by adding the ricotz/testing ppa to upgrade to his (then) current package.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-25
Found this [link]("http://community.linuxmint.com/tutorial/view/198") over on the Linux Mint facebook page, gives instructions on how to build LMDE from scratch, using Debian.  Anyhow, the thing that fascinated me was the liquorix kernel, so I had to install it, and I have to say I am impressed so far, this thing is quick.

These are the instructions for the 64-bit version but with a little browsing I'm sure it could be adapted for the 32-bit version.

  **Liquorix**
   I also want to point out Liquorix. It's a kernel built upon the Zen  Kernel sources, designed for desktop usage over server usage. You'll  notice that Debian's default kernels are optimized for servers  (basically, they're optimized for thoroughput over latency), and you  want a kernel that feels more responsive and faster. The Liquorix  kernels are also more up-to-date than the kernels you'll find in Debian  Testing, so it's a lazy but effective way of running a new kernel but  not having the patience or time to compile a newer kernel yourself. :P
```
   $ gksu gedit /etc/apt/sources.list
   Append "deb http://liquorix.net/debian sid main" (yes, "sid") at the bottom of your sources.list. Save and close Gedit.
   $ sudo apt-get install liquorix-keyring
   (Fetch Liquorix's keyrings)
   $ sudo apt-get install linux-headers-2.6-liquorix-amd64 linux-image-2.6-liquorix-amd64
   And reboot into your new kernel after you're done to give it a try.

```I also did the **export CLUTTER_VBLANK=none** trick in the ~/.profile file the other day, I know it's supposed to be an ATI fix but on my Nvidia card it seems faster.

If any of you try this let me know what you think.

Here is a link straight to the liquorix website with a clear set of instructions [http://liquorix.net/](http://liquorix.net/)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-06-30
[How-to on pinning a software version using apt or synaptic]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/PinningHowto")

So you can do a regular apt-get upgrade without overwriting a selected version of software.

For instance, I don't want to overwrite my 2.32 version of gdm that I downgraded to using synaptic.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-02
Ok, didn't have the time to pinpoint it but if you want your alt-f2 functionality back you will need to purge the ricotz/testing ppa, then re-install & reactivate it long enough to install the network manager files.  This will also affect two extensions, the alternate status (power off option) & righthotcorner.

You can get the power off functionality back by removing alternate status and installing Murphy's poweroptions extension from his [repository]("http://www.fpmurphy.com/gnome-shell-extensions/") . (Either versions 1.0 or 2.0 should work)

Personally I've grown fond of the righthotcorner extension & I use Synapse so I've opted to stay current with the ricotz/testing ppa on my installation.  If you go with the purge/reinstall option I recommend deactivating it to maintain a more stable installation.

---

### Post by bulldog on 2011-07-03
> **cbowman57 said:**
> Ok, didn't have the time to pinpoint it but if you want your alt-f2 functionality back you will need to purge the ricotz/testing ppa, then re-install & reactivate it long enough to install the network manager files.  This will also affect two extensions, the alternate status (power off option) & righthotcorner.

You can get the power off functionality back by removing alternate status and installing Murphy's poweroptions extension from his [repository]("http://www.fpmurphy.com/gnome-shell-extensions/") . (Either versions 1.0 or 2.0 should work)

Personally I've grown fond of the righthotcorner extension & I use Synapse so I've opted to stay current with the ricotz/testing ppa on my installation.  If you go with the purge/reinstall option I recommend deactivating it to maintain a more stable installation.

Some questions,

1/Can't you enable the ALT-F2 in the system settings again
2/How about the pink lines in some gtk3 themes,are they still there.
3/No ricotz in use but still have the alternate status menu and the right hot corner working.
4/Can you tell what extra the ricotz provide,which you don't have without the ricotz ppa.

Want to know if enable the ricotz ppa brings some benefits,or just make the chance of breaking your install a lot bigger.

Thanks in advance.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-03
> **libihero said:**
> well there is an icon in the message tray...
it seems like im having the exact opposite problem as you lol, i can hear alerts for messages (dont really see anything), but no convos will appear in the message tray

Think I've found the empathy fix.  Try disabling the FixCSS extension.  Every update to gnome-shell seems to affect the operation of some of the base extensions.  I added the FixCSS extension to correct a problem with the shell displaying LARGE icons on the top panel, it might no longer be needed.

Update:  Didn't seem to fix it but still a good idea to disable the FixCSS extension if your display looks alright.

### Post by tigull on 2011-07-06
Hello, I would just like to know how you ported Unity to Gnome3. Maybe you've answered this question already but I was getting lost in the 100+ pages of the thread. Thanks for your time!

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-06
> **tigull said:**
> Hello, I would just like to know how you ported Unity to Gnome3. Maybe you've answered this question already but I was getting lost in the 100+ pages of the thread. Thanks for your time!

Unity is exactly as Canonical & Ubuntu made it, no porting required.   This was simply Ubuntu 11.04, with gnome 3.0 shell added to the mix.  The gnome3-team & Rico (ricotz ppa) are responsible for packaging Gnome 3.0 for Ubuntu and getting it to coexist with Unity on the same installation.  The UGR team created the metapackage that simplifies adding Gnome 3.0 by installing & removing certain packages to create a Gnome shell desktop virtually identical to other distros.

For a more technical answer you would probably need to direct your question to somebody on one of those teams.  Other than the chit-chat and troubleshooting the links to UGR & the recipe are in post #1.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-09
> **cecilpierce said:**
> no themes show up with the 3.1.3 gs ???

Hmm, did you update gnome-shell-extension-user-themes?  If not, you can navigate to /usr/share/gnome-shell/extensions and edit the metadata.json to 3.1.3 in that one and it will probably work.  It worked for me the other night when I was playing around.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-10
@Keith; I use Clonezilla, I just back up the partition.

I've got it configured to boot from an iso on another partition so it loads like it's on USB or disk. I can access it from the grub menu.  I also mount/bind my Music, Downloads, Pictures & a Maintenance partition so all I'm really backing up is less than 5Gb.

---

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-07-10
Ok thanks .... not sure ***[mount/bind]("http://aplawrence.com/Linux/mount_bind.html")*** will work for me as I am constantly swapping USB drives around to go to other systems ..... but will look at how [***clonezilla***]("http://clonezilla.org/") works. ty

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-10
> **23dornot23d said:**
> Ok thanks .... not sure ***[mount/bind]("http://aplawrence.com/Linux/mount_bind.html")*** will work for me as I am constantly swapping USB drives around to go to other systems ..... but will look at how [***clonezilla***]("http://clonezilla.org/") works. ty

If you decide to give it a try with the USB make sure you use UUID= instead of dev/sdxx and possibly **auto** in the fstab.

This doesn't use auto, & not sure if it's perfect but it works.

```
UUID=528d7ac7-4fc9-432a-9244-ae3433e498ae  /mnt/bind/Pictures    ext4  rw,noatime,nodiratime   0  2
/mnt/bind/Pictures                         /home/chuck/Pictures  none  defaults,bind           0  0

```Clonezilla works great.

### Post by ironcrush on 2011-07-11
Hi first of all thanks for nice this effort. Been trying to get the GNOME 3 working on my system for long time but no luck at all! 
Installed gNatty Gnome Live ISO v3.1 on my Toshiba Satllite-A200, Intel Centrino-Duo, 2GB Ram, nVIDIA Go7300 graphics( where the main problem is). Burnt the iso on a usb stick, Couldn't get GNOME 3 session on Live mode. So installed it on another usb stick(16GB), logged into gnome classic(as GNOME 3 wouldn't work on installed system either), installed NVIDIA-Linux-x86-275.09.07 rebooted system, tried to logged into GNOME 3, Got the desktop with all the extensions, can move the cursor using touchpad BUT CANNOT CLICK ON ANYTHING!! No respons from the desktop from anywhere. I can get to the another terminal using ctrl+alt+F2 that mean keyboard and mouse( as I can move the cursor) are functional. 
Has anyone had the same issue?
Any help with that please??
My Xorg and nvidia is running ok, I can run Gnome classic with no problem.
Right now running system update, hoping it would fix something :)
Cheers

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-12
> **munkymac said:**
> Okay, here's the screenshot. Obviously I'm in this mess because i left the ricotz/testing ppa enabled. Now I'm greeted with a Partial Upgrade notice every time Update Manager pops up. Should I wait until the Partial Upgrade problem works itself out before disabling ricotz?

You've probably already done this a dozen times but double check the edit you did to metadata.json, lg is indicating that it's a version problem so it's not even trying to load. 

These extensions are new to me too so there is a lot to learn about them.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-12
> **ironcrush said:**
> Hi first of all thanks for nice this effort. Been trying to get the GNOME 3 working on my system for long time but no luck at all! 
Installed gNatty Gnome Live ISO v3.1 on my Toshiba Satllite-A200, Intel Centrino-Duo, 2GB Ram, nVIDIA Go7300 graphics( where the main problem is). Burnt the iso on a usb stick, Couldn't get GNOME 3 session on Live mode. So installed it on another usb stick(16GB), logged into gnome classic(as GNOME 3 wouldn't work on installed system either), installed NVIDIA-Linux-x86-275.09.07 rebooted system, tried to logged into GNOME 3, Got the desktop with all the extensions, can move the cursor using touchpad BUT CANNOT CLICK ON ANYTHING!! No respons from the desktop from anywhere. I can get to the another terminal using ctrl+alt+F2 that mean keyboard and mouse( as I can move the cursor) are functional. 
Has anyone had the same issue?
Any help with that please??
My Xorg and nvidia is running ok, I can run Gnome classic with no problem.
Right now running system update, hoping it would fix something :)
Cheers

First off I'd check to see that nVidia driver 173 is uninstalled, you should be able to do that from additional drivers.  Personally I've had problems with the 275.09.06 & .07 drivers so I downgraded to 270.41.06 which doesn't cause any problems for me.

Now something I have no experience with are the intel/nVidia video chipsets.  I'm wondering if you need to blacklist a driver?  Something I also know nothing about, but I'm sure somebody is familiar with the process.  I'd start by making sure 173 is uninstalled, then if there are still problems downgrade to 270.41.06.

Don't sweat the partial upgrade thing, but you might want to make sure that in synaptic, under updates, you have proposed activated.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-13
@ironcrush; I've had that problem with some Oneiric pre-alphas, but it affected Unity & Gnome fallback.  This problem is just in gnome-shell correct?  Have you updated the system since you installed it?

---

### Post by ironcrush on 2011-07-13
Yes its only in gnome-shell. Well.. I installed the system then installed nvidia driver from a offline file, didn't enable internet, so i didn't do any update for the first time. I got the gnome-shell ok but no response, but could get a working desktop on gnome-classic mode. So only then i thought i would try updating the system, still no luck. Getting a bit frustrated at this point now :)

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-13
> **ironcrush said:**
> Yes its only in gnome-shell. Well.. I installed the system then installed nvidia driver from a offline file, didn't enable internet, so i didn't do any update for the first time. I got the gnome-shell ok but no response, but could get a working desktop on gnome-classic mode. So only then i thought i would try updating the system, still no luck. Getting a bit frustrated at this point now :)

I can understand the frustration, it's just odd that it only affects gnome-shell, not Unity or classic gnome.  A problem with gtk 3.0 or mutter is what I'm thinking at this point.

Well, time to get drastic I guess, if you already turned off ricotz/testing turn it back on and see if an upgrade fixes it.

---

### Post by munkymac on 2011-07-14
> You've probably already done this a dozen times but double check the edit you did to metadata.json, lg is indicating that it's a version problem so it's not even trying to load.

In trying every iteration, I think I may have left it on a version below 3.1.3. Here's the new screenshot with the metadata.json checked, fixed, doublechecked, and then checked again.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-14
Alright, that did change things.  From what I've been reading lately Gnome 3.2 is supposed to have integrated chat and the Empathy notifications have been broken for awhile now.  That extension is trying to make a call to something that doesn't seem to exist in the shell any longer.  Maybe the author has done an update on the extension?

### Post by Redsandro on 2011-07-14
This is important stuff you guys do here. Ubuntu has taken one too many bad decisions and I was just about to switch to Fedora. The thing that stopped me is that the iso failed to boot on my target machine. I didn't really want to switch, but I don't want to un-cripple a distro before use either. I hope (this real) GNOME Ubuntu becomes GNUbuntu, and I hope it becomes more popular than normal crippled ignorant chaotic kiddy Unitybuntu.

Curiosity: Why is it so hard to get (real) GNOME + shell in Ubuntu? The UGR team does a good job too, but in a perfect world, all you'd have to do is apt-get some stuff.

I remember the very first time; adding a PPA, apt-get, dist-upgrade and by accident everything just worked. God was annoyed by this hassle-free transition and said: It shall never be this easy again anymore! 

> **cbowman57 said:**
> my connection is painfully slow so it needs all the help it can get.

In the Netherlands, the torrent downloads at 50 KB/s, in America at 3 MB/s, wut? And I thought internet was global. :P

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-15

[http://files.Redsandro.com/ubuntu/gNatty Gnome amd64 v3 Live.iso]("http://files.redsandro.com/ubuntu/gNatty%20Gnome%20amd64%20v3%20Live/gNatty%20Gnome%20amd64%20v3%20Live.iso") (64 bit)

I really don't know why it's so hard, wouldn't have to be if Ubuntu wanted it to work but they are putting all their chips on Unity.  This is essentially the iso that UGR hasn't released yet, I don't take credit for anything but using their recipe & adding ricotz/testing (which if not deactivated after installation will jump you to gnome-shell 3.1.3 which will break some things).

I hope it works out of the box for you and doesn't create any headaches.  Let me know how it goes tomorrow.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-16
Just a reminder, if you leave ricotz/testing active it will upgrade your gnome-shell to 3.1.3 (unstable).  The update today in particular broke quite a few extensions, the result is that you will not be able to login to gnome-shell without removing the offending extensions.

For the best Gnome 3.0 shell experience on Ubuntu make sure you disable ppa:ricotz/testing and stick with gnome-shell 3.0.2 until Gnome gets closer to 3.2 and extensions are re-written to support it.

Basically, if you want to leave ricotz/testing active I won't be able to provide a whole lot of advice or support.

---

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-07-18
Still here ..... just upgraded one of my systems to 3.1.3 using the gdebi and the latest ricotz gnome-shell ......... keeping everything else held back for the time being though.

[[COLOR=Red]**BURG**[/COLOR]]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Burg") was ok when it first came out ..... but since GRUB2 is pretty well sorted from what it was like when it first came out - me I would tend
to stick with GRUB2 as there are more chances of any problems being addressed IMHO - than with the BURG ( *[BURG FORUM]("http://www.burgloader.com/bbs/index.php?PHPSESSID=23ullq83geru6klqb9of7u4nf4&")* ) ..... although it was pretty good when I tried it out ..... nice menu displays etc .... only ever installed  it to a spare flash drive though.

For information [[COLOR=Red]**Super Boot Manager **[/COLOR]]("http://www.addictivetips.com/ubuntu-linux-tips/manage-plymouth-burg-grub-with-super-boot-manager/")
You are almost right on the vacation thing except it works out the opposite for me here .... 
people come across here on vacation - then I end up becoming the tour guide / helper for the time they are over here ..... been fun though ......
also taken a few photos .... but of things my friend is having to get rid of [photos]("https://sites.google.com/site/resizephotostopost/home/photo-display-cabinets") )

Also been doing quite a lot of work using Wings3D and Blender ..... trying to sort an old lean to out .... so modelled it up to do a layout
would like it as an external wet room with shower toilet sink in it . 
( having problems with reality though .... can shrink and grow things to suit on the 3D system on computer .... but when I cut plasterboard wrong size
it just refuses to expand to fit in the gaps ...... :(   ) 

Watching this thread on a night time - the remarks made about it are all pointing to similar things that I encountered as I tried upgrading  .....

The Adwaita theme went walksbouts on one upgrade  .... and I had to replace it .... seen that as a GDM problem on Oneiric ..... once replaced and put the Theme back it was ok for me though ..... ( why the Unity upgrade removed it - again is another question - just because Unity does not use it !!! )

The not so smiley face appearing that was a while back now ..... but was related to one of the extensions messing up ..... but that is ok now .... 
( to avoid it you can run Gnome-Shell from LXDE ...... doing [COLOR=Red]**gnome-shell --replace**[/COLOR] seems to work better for some reason )
or even do it from Unity ..... and then things seem to be ok - not sure why though :confused:

If you lock all the packages to do with gnome-shell ...... then it seems to go well
this is what I have currently done to get it to remain stable.

Its all looking good on my installs at the moment though ..... and I am now using it as a production machine ..... ( actually 3.0.1 is my main system and is extremely stable for me )

Just be careful when choosing what you are going to upgrade ...... and read around the forum often someone will encounter a problem and shortly afterwards a solution will be found - such as the linking of the applications.menu ..... 
( Why it was altered is another question though )

Also of course the version numbers for 3.1.3 ...... maybe a dependency should be the gNatty Pack 3.1.3 with all the versions changed to suit.
( that would save everyone else having to do it ..... but obviously this is always going to be a problem with version increases ) 

( I never let the system decide my upgrades ..... nowadays >> mainly its just the applications and any dependencies that I allow to be upgraded as I go along ) ...... 

But I will be looking forward to 3.2 though

@peyu; after some looking around it seems that the VLC screensaver problem is a known bug with gnome shell currently.

If you watch a lot of video you might want to install the presentation mode extension.  You'll have to compile it from git [https://github.com/ProGEEK/Presentation-Tools#readme](https://github.com/ProGEEK/Presentation-Tools#readme)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-20
@Rasa1111; you need to change your Windows > Current theme setting with Gnome Tweak Tool (Tweak Advanced Settings under Applications)

[ATTACH]197933[/ATTACH]

Don't know what that will do to your buttons on left but I'm pretty sure you can reset that with Ubuntu Tweak.

You need to **alt+f2** type **r** then press **enter** to see the change take effect.

Oh, the default is Adwaita.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-21
@peyu;  Yes, there were some significant changes made to 3.1.3 in preparation for 3.2 that broke some of the extensions.  What I bundled were just a collection I found useful & thought most Gnome 2.0 users would miss.  Glad you found a weather extension that worked for you, hopefully when 3.2 arrives we'll have some more extension authors, I wish I had the knack but it passed me by.  One shortcoming we have using Ubuntu, at least for now, is that most of the extensions are being made by people using other distros, Fedora primarily from what I'm seeing & I don't think any other distros are using 3.1.3 yet.

You could do a ppa-purge of ricotz/testing and it would bring you back to a more stable release.

### Post by peyu on 2011-07-21
i'm running since yesterday 3.1.3 and everything is ok, except the battery indicator (which was not working either in 3.0) and the weather extension that i had to compile. 
About libgnome-desktop-3-2, I had the same problem of dependencies. You need to fix the verson of a dependent package, but i don't remember its name. The way to do it is to install only the package that synaptic complain about, then synaptic will tells you which dependent package is problematic. If you repeat this process, at the end synaptic will complain about a wrong package version. You just have to fix this package version to the right one and it will work.

change-theme  fixed        Makefile  scalable  symlinks.tar.gz
configure     index.theme  README    src
keithg@keith-Aspire-7730ZG:/usr/share/icons/Clarity$ 


```Edit
Yep that does it ..... as it rebuilds them ..... 

[IMG]http://img692.imageshack.us/img692/5764/rebuildiconsr.jpg[/IMG]

gksu Nautilus
and 
1. go to the directory /usr/share/icons/Clarity
**2. double click change-theme**
3. select the colour you want ..... it will rebuild the icon theme and log out login and it works ....

[[COLOR=Red]_***SCREENSHOT***_[/COLOR]]("http://img708.imageshack.us/img708/5390/screenshotat20110724211.jpg") - chose dark_canus I think ..... its just slightly darker but it did change it .........

### Post by el_koraco on 2011-07-24
For Awoken, you need to follow the steps outlined on the Gnome look page, about cp-ing folders to /../icons nad the customization tool to /usr/bin. The you run the script. Worked on my Fedora install. You'll probably want the Awoken dark icons with the bright theme. It's in the tarball.

### Post by wlake on 2011-07-24
I'm running Oneiric with Gnome Shell. I've recently gotten some extensions to work that wouldn't work before, (such as panel favorites) by substituting this metadata.json for the original one. The only change I see (besides the shell-version) is the extra entry for "localedir". I've only tested this on panel favorites and move clock but it worked for both. I would be curious to know if this works for anybody else. I got this metadata.json from one of the 3.1.3 extensions that came with Oneiric.
{
 "uuid": "Panel_Favorites@rmy.pobox.com",
 "name": "Panel Favorites",
 "description": "Add launchers for Favorites to the panel",
 "shell-version": [ "3.1.3" ],
 "localedir": "/usr/share/locale",
 "url": "http://intgat.tigress.co.uk/rmy/extensions/index.html"
}

Regards,

### Post by cecilpierce on 2011-07-26
@bulldog

40_custom in /etc/burg.d/40_custom:

#!/bin/sh
exec tail -n +3 $0
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
menuentry 'Natty 11.04 (sda1)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os --group gro
up_main {
        recordfail
        set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
        insmod part_msdos
        insmod ext2
        set root='(hd0,1)'
        linux   /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda1 ro quiet splash nomce vt.handoff=7
        initrd  /initrd.img
}

This works for burg or grub and boots the latest kernel, whatever @vmlinuz points to in the root dir

### Post by sanderd17 on 2011-07-26
I was on gNatty v2, 32-bit. And I saw that a "partial distribution upgrade" was possible via the update manager. I know those things are dangerous, but I thought I would just try it. The upgrade installed fine, GS still loads. But it doesn't connect to any network (not wireless or wired). I just get a message that my device isn't supported on this "version". I don't know if they mean Ubuntu version or Gnome version, but it's shown with such an "Ooops" Gnome Face.

As a result, I don't know if it's the fault of Ubuntu (a problem with Oneiric maybe) or the fault of Gnome.

I'm now on a Mint 11 installation that I had still installed (always good to have a backup OS :D).

Did anyone else see those problems? Or something like it?

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-07-26
> 
As a result, I don't know if it's the fault of Ubuntu (a problem with Oneiric maybe) or the fault of Gnome.
Oneiric is way too unstable at this moment in time for Gnome-Shell ...... too many risks if you are trying to
run a reasonably stable system ...... 

I usually wait till later on nearer the end of development phase to start testing changes out ...... but did have a go
right at the start of this one .... and was quite surprised how well it was going for the first few weeks ......

Then I lost .... desktop startup application launchers .... then cairo-dock .... then transparency ..... but read about problems with the network manager .... so never went there ..... think what killed it for me was having to run it
from another desktop to get it anything like stable ..... 

used LXDE .... then did gnome-shell --replace 

Which kept it going quite a bit longer ..... cannot remember exactly what killed it off for me ......

But in the end I went back to 11.04 ..... then added the Oneiric repositories and use aptitude safe-upgrade to
keep updating ....... ( not sure this is what the developers would advise ,,,,, but I have done it this way right from using kanotix .... and upgraded through a few Ubuntu releases too ..... this you obviously do at your own risk though as there could be all sorts of problems ...... but the dependencies are pretty good at throwing out bogus things nowadays .... at the moment mine is running the best its ever run ...... )

If it was a mechanical device it would be similar to [[COLOR=Red]_***Heath Robinsons***_[/COLOR]]("http://www.google.com/search?q=Heath+Robinson&hl=en&client=ubuntu&hs=OTn&channel=fs&prmd=ivnso&tbm=isch&tbo=u&source=univ&sa=X&ei=ogkvTuHvPM22hAf0rphC&ved=0CDMQsAQ&biw=1152&bih=547") designs ..... ;)

Mixing and matching Gnome 2 and Gnome 3 may not be the greatest idea ..... but if they stick to the same calls in programs and naming conventions .... then hopefully it will run ok as it is doing ..... as I say this at the moment is the best mine has ever run and so fast too .... temps are lower with the 3.0.0-6 kernel and the later graphics driver too .....

Just removed ubuntuone again as I do not use it and why it keeps trying to sync when I do not use it I do not know ,,,,,,
its history now though ,,,,, removed any packages to do with it rebooted ,,,,

All is much better now and the fan as calmed itself .... and is also faster to boot in ....

I know, I quit reading other posts in the forum because of the bullheaded notion that Unity & GS can't co-exist, provide tons of proof to the contrary and people will still say "If you want to use gnome shell you'll have to go to Fedora or Arch".  Duh!! :)

I hadn't had much luck either with Debian distros until LMDE paved the way.  Now things seem easier and I'm becoming increasingly familiar with the "Debian way" of doing things.  Really miss an app like Ubuntu Tweak, would really like to see the authors of Ubuntu Tweak & Gnome tweak tool put their heads together, the result would be awesome.

Well, still downloading packages for the repository experiment.

You know, thinking about it, a local repo for Oneiric testers might not be a bad idea in case you want to downgrade to a nonexistent package.  From synaptic, look at 'Status' > 'Installed' select all for reinstallation but don't apply, instead click on 'File' and generate a download script.  Name it, save it & move it to an empty folder and execute it.  It will download the deb for everything installed on your system. (hopefully) :)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-29
Well after trying mintconstructor, which is very limited in comparison to UCK, I found a way to get Remastersys working.  So, I made a distro iso that I managed to install, it's got some bugs but I might fiddle with it some more and see what I can do.  Nowhere near ready for a public trial but I thought I'd let you know what I've been up to.

Anyway, it's only 64-bit, not sure if there will ever be a 32-bit version.  It's LMDE dist-upgrade(d) to Debian unstable (Sid) with a really good Gnome 3.0 shell running on it.  Here's a shot with Elementary shell theme, Mint-X window theme & a default Linux Mint background & basically all the gNatty Pack extensions.

[ATTACH]198788[/ATTACH]

If this project does gain any steam I will start a fresh thread to discuss it. :twisted:

Anyway, it's only 64-bit, not sure if there will ever be a 32-bit version.  It's LMDE dist-upgrade(d) to Debian unstable (wheezy?) with a really good Gnome 3.0 shell running on it.

Not sure if anyone's tried that yet, it's an awesome idea. It will break beyond horrible at some point, but so would Oneiric.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-29
> **el_koraco said:**
> Testing is Wheezy, unstable is always Sid. So, you basically have Linux Mint Sid. :D

Not sure if anyone's tried that yet, it's an awesome idea. It will break beyond horrible at some point, but so would Oneiric.

Ok, that always confuses me, thanks for clearing it up for me.

It runs great, rivals gNatty and I like the fact that I don't have to work around Unity.  I was stuck in fallback mode for a few days, but that wasn't such a bad thing.  I learned that the panels can be manipulated some by doing alt+r-click so for those missing Gnome 2 it can become even more familiar.  Of course for anybody that is used to Debian it's a simple matter to "freeze" their installation by deactivating some repos.

For user themes & common extension support I did it the easy way, convert the Fedora .rpm's with Alien, simple code so it converted clean.

I pretty much do at least a daily Clonezilla backup for the times it gets broken.  But hey, if something doesn't break I'm not pushing it hard enough. :)

deb [http://ftp.us.debian.org/debian](http://ftp.us.debian.org/debian) unstable main contrib non-free
deb [http://ftp.us.debian.org/debian](http://ftp.us.debian.org/debian) experimental main contrib non-free

[/LIST]

/etc/apt/apt.conf.d/local


[LIST]
[*]```
APT::Default-Release "sid";
```
[/LIST]

**[How to install GNOME 3 on Debian testing aka wheezy]("http://raphaelhertzog.com/2011/06/16/installing-gnome-3-on-debian-6-0-squeeze-no-sorry/")** (The article I started with)

Probably going to take some trial & error because I really don't remember everything I did to get here.  You might find it much easier than I did. :)

If you decide to try and make a distro out of it I suggest you don't tweak it with smxi or add the liquorix kernel.  The error I made, that screwed me on a simple distro was removing all the excess video modules.  Pretty sure that the only reason I was able to install it (the Remastersys distro iso) was because I had the proprietary nvidia driver installed that matched my system.

If you have any questions I'll do my best to answer them but I don't know how much I remember. :)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-07-29
Yeah, if you're going for your own personal installation go ahead & grab the smxi script which simplifies Debian a great deal, but it's not fancy gui.

You can google it but if you want a headstart: ```
cd /usr/local/bin && wget -Nc smxi.org/smxi.zip && unzip smxi.zip && smxi
```

You're probably safe to use the script & add liquorix for a distro but if you go that route don't remove the extra video modules.  You'll figure it out when you run it. :)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-08-06
Rasa,

Ok, so you haven't done any upgrades since installing it?   Let's get that done, then hopefuly it will take care of your first question.

Best way to manage it is to use aptitude.  I think it's installed, if not install it with synaptic or the command line.

Then run aptitude update && aptitude safe-upgrade.  Should work out, if you run into anything strange post a shot and I'll try to advise you.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-08-09
Thought you guys might like to read a little something about SweetTooth.

**SweetTooth's Slow Progress**

    
[LIST]
[*]*From*: "Jasper St. Pierre" <jstpierre mecheye net>
[*]*To*: gnome-shell-list gnome org, desktop-devel-list gnome org
[*]*Subject*: SweetTooth's Slow Progress
[*]*Date*: Sat, 23 Jul 2011 02:12:07 -0400
[/LIST]
      Hey guys, it's Jasper.

The last time I talked  about  SweetTooth[0] it was about the live enabling/disabling extensions  proposal. I've been meaning to follow-up on this ML thread, but I was  sidetracked: a bit annoyed with resizing on my new laptop, I started  working on invisible borders in mutter, as a side-project. After a  little while, this has been my main effort for a few weeks. Thankfully,  this is almost about to land. The changes there also allowed for a  simple implementation of anti-aliased corners, making gnome-shell look  extremely nice! [1] See BGO bugs #644930 [2] and #628195 [3] for the  implementation.
    
Additionally, I was waiting on getting the Shell changes in  upstream. I had built up a number of patches for gnome-shell in my local  stack, and wanted to land some of these before getting further I  initially filed these as four or five different bug reports, which ended  up being unwieldy. The new bug, BGO #654770 [4], has all the new  patches for your eyes to review.
    
Before this is reviewed and landed, I want to make sure that  everybody here will be able to port their extensions to the new API. See  the old, invalid BGO bug #635209 [5] for a quick overview and the  previous ML post [6] for a more in-depth take on it. Given that these  are up and fairly stable, next Monday I'm going to start working on  these features, besides some general polish:
  
  1. Writing and testing forward-compatibility in case I have to  break the DBus API in the future. The website has to work from 3.2  onward for the rest of its life, even if most of the extensions don't.  Using require.js and some clever hacking, I have this done and working  in my local tree.
 
  2. Extension code review. For now, this will probably be a secure  link to a .zip file with the code and a yay/nay with a comment option.

  3. After a crash, the fail-whale dialog will show you your enabled  extensions with an option to disable them before restarting the Shell.

  4. Detecting a mismatch between your current Shell version and an  extension's version, and prevent you from shooting yourself in  the foot installing an out-of-date plugin.

  5. If an extension errors out, I'll be adding an option to mail the stack trace to the author.

Unfortunately,  the delay means that I probably can't guarantee anything other than the  features on this list in for 3.2. That said, if there's concerns or  questions about this giant trainwreck, feel free to yell and/or ask.

  Jasper

[http://mail.gnome.org/archives/desktop-devel-list/2011-July/msg00033.html](http://mail.gnome.org/archives/desktop-devel-list/2011-July/msg00033.html)

Anyone know the command to bring up the shutdown gui? It used to be "gnome-session-save --shutdown-dialog", but that's not installed in gnome3 so I'm guessing gnome3 is different.

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-08-23
There probably is but if you look at the .desktop files, here's an example I extracted from the gnome-dictionary.desktop file

```
Exec=gnome-dictionary
Terminal=false
Type=Application
Icon=accessories-dictionary
StartupNotify=true
Categories=GNOME;GTK;Office;Dictionary;
NotShowIn=KDE;XFCE;
X-GNOME-DocPath=gnome-dictionary/gnome-dictionary.xml
X-GNOME-Bugzilla-Bugzilla=GNOME
X-GNOME-Bugzilla-Product=gnome-utils
X-GNOME-Bugzilla-Component=gdict
X-GNOME-Bugzilla-Version=3.0.1
```

If you look at some others, or google it, you might find more information about what DE to show an application in, there may be a **ShowIn=** entry, I'm not sure.  If you're trying to control what applications are displayed in different DE's the **NotShownIn=** entry is probably what you want.

16th of July 2011, it is working really nice, sometimes the login striked but after the next reboot it was always working. Maybe this has something to do with the accountservice which got an update yesterday. 
Thanks a lot for building the iso.  :guitar:

I also want to report some things I did after the install:

At first I did not check the [http://ppa.launchpad.net/ricotz/testing/ubuntu](http://ppa.launchpad.net/ricotz/testing/ubuntu) for updating

About three days before today as your iso had been installed I did

```

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get upgrade
sudo apt-get dist-upgrade

```

after that gnome-shell was gone for some reason and then was reinstalled by

```

sudo apt-get install gnome-shell

```

after a reboot the possibilty to choose gnome was also gone and at first
I was a little disappointed but then just remembered about the fallback
package I noticed a time ago during some reading and only gave a try to

```

sudo apt-get install gnome-session-fallback

```

and then after the next reboot I was back at gnome3.
It is working great and all is up to date at the moment 

Maybe this information could be helpful in some way.

---

### Post by grubu on 2011-08-23
Okay I think that is why we got such a nice build from you.
I have to say that I played a little with Fedora but at the moment gnome3 is working very well here at your distro. Yes the gnome3 really got its fascinating also to me. Although I like the xfce4 very much and sometimes just switch to it, gnome3 will be more and more used. Today I told a windows-xp friend of mine about some nice gnome3 features which are useful and that he needs to give it a try. Maybe some weeks ago I would rate him to install Fedora but at the moment I would just bring your iso to impress him. :P

By the way it really boots nice and fast with grub2 by just:

```

menuentry 'gNatty cbowman-s Gnome3 amd64 v3 Live CD ISO' {
isofile=gnatty-amd64-v3.iso
loopback loop (hd0,8)/isofilesdirectory/$isofile
linux (loop)/casper/vmlinuz boot=casper 
iso-scan/filename=/isofilesdirectory/$isofile noprompt noeject nosplash quiet locale=en_US bootkbd=us console-setup/layoutcode=us gfxpayload=1024x768x16
initrd (loop)/casper/initrd.lz
}

```in my  /etc/grub.d/40_custom  file; it was also installed onto another partition after booting

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-08-26
Today's lesson learned:

If you're going to put blockdev --setra 8192 /dev/sd<x> in /etc/rc.local make sure it has the path to blockdev, ie. /sbin/blockdev

I feel like an idiot. :)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-08-26
Found this link, might help you sort out that applications display problem. Not certain but might be worth looking at. 

[https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/GNOME_Tips#gnome-system-tools](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/GNOME_Tips#gnome-system-tools)

> 
You may want to configure system-wide default applications and file  associations. This is extremely useful when you have some KDE  applications installed, but still prefer a GNOME ones to be launched by  default. 
To do that you can install [gnome-defaults-list]("http://aur.archlinux.org/packages.php?ID=23170") from AUR. It will place your configuration file at [COLOR=#005500][FONT=monospace]/etc/gnome/defaults.list[/FONT][/COLOR]. 
If you want to do everything manually, create [COLOR=#005500][FONT=monospace]/usr/share/applications/defaults.list[/FONT][/COLOR] with the following format: 
 [Default Applications] 
application/pdf=evince.desktop
 image/jpeg=eog.desktop
 ... That info was a little dated, take a look at this:

> 
**  How to remove application entries from the Applications view **

 Like many other Desktop Environments, GNOME 3 uses the .desktop files to populate its Applications view. Those files are located in */usr/share/applications*. Note that Nautilus does not display them as text files, thus it is not  possible to edit them. Use a terminal to display the real files. 
 $ ls /usr/share/applications  For system wide changes, edit the files directly from there. For local changes, make a copy in your home folder: 
 $ cp /usr/share/applications/* ~/.local/share/applications/  You can edit the .desktop files to fit your wishes. Note that  removing a .desktop file will not uninstall the application but will  mostly remove the desktop integration (MIME-Types, shortcuts, and so  on). Add the following entry to the .desktop file to hide the shortcut from  the Applications view: 
 $ echo "NoDisplay=true" >> foo.desktop [https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/GNOME](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/GNOME)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-09-01
Restoring the old Delete key behavior.  This isn't my fix, and I've seen it other blogs & articles but the last time I tried it would not work.  This method did.

Stolen from the Arch forum

> 
Quick fix:
Run dconf-editor, and open:
org -> gnome -> desktop -> interface
Enable "can-change-accels".
Open nautilus, select any file/directory, then click "Edit" from menubar, and hover on "Move to Trash" menuitem.
While hovering, click on your delete key. The accel should change from "cntrl+del" to "del".
Make sure you have selected a file, else the "Move to Trash" menuitem will be greyed out.
I suggest you disable "can-change-accels" afterwards, to prevent accidental accel changes.

If it didn't work, create an empty folder ~/.gnome2/accels & try again.

### Post by aeronutt on 2011-09-02
FYI, if 11.04 and GS is working for you, I wouldn't rush to Oneiric if you're concerned about battery power. Here are what my numbers are showing.

Natty, (11.04): ~950ma
Oneiric alpha 3 (11.10): ~1500ma
Oneiric beta 1 (11.10): ~2350ma

So, Oneiric beta is 2.5x the battery current that Natty is using. Wow.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-09-04
From the Natty side use GParted or the command line & check the disk [(http://linux.die.net/man/8/fsck)]("http://linux.die.net/man/8/fsck")
 make sure that partition isn't mounted, I akways just use GParted but it can be done with fsck too.

That's where I'd start.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-09-04
Ok, go into your working installation, mount your gNatty partition. In the root directory of the gNatty partition create an empty file /forcefsck [(http://www.kernelhardware.org/linux-force-disk-check-fsck/)]("http://www.kernelhardware.org/linux-force-disk-check-fsck/")

Reboot to your gNatty installation and it will run the check & fix any errors.

This might not be the problem but it's where you need to start.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-09-05
Yeah, one of the reasons Arch & a few others don't use a splash.

From the grub screen, have you tried the recovery menu entries?  You could also try editing the boot entry, remove everything that looks like quiet or splash, if it's a plymouth problem that might boot.

Keith makes a good suggestion, but I would just 
sudo apt-get purge plymouth*
sudo apt-get install --reinstall gdm3 (or just gdm)

---

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2011-09-27
> **ryanrio95 said:**
> Thanks, i thought so that this is the problem.
Why didn't you include the nvidia-current package in the livecd. This could solve this problem i think.

Greets, Ryan.

Size constraints, and it seems like you fix one glitch and it creates a problem for 10 other people.  By staying with Ubuntu's installation and keeping it as stock as possible it was hoped that problems would be avoided, or at least none introduced.

Basically I was damned if I did & damned if I didn't. :)

You might be better off by going to the command line & sudo apt-get purge nvidia-current, then go back to the desktop & used Additional Drivers to install the latest one.

---

### Post by ryanrio95 on 2011-09-28
> **cbowman57 said:**
> Size constraints, and it seems like you fix one glitch and it creates a problem for 10 other people.  By staying with Ubuntu's installation and keeping it as stock as possible it was hoped that problems would be avoided, or at least none introduced.

Basically I was damned if I did & damned if I didn't. :)

You might be better off by going to the command line & sudo apt-get purge nvidia-current, then go back to the desktop & used Additional Drivers to install the latest one.

Have you installed the gnome-session-fallback package into the live cd?
Because when this is installed you don't need 3D drivers because Ubuntu has an Fallback option in the gnome-session files. And there is set that when gnome-shell can't load you will automatically be redirected to the fallback mode, within this mode you can download the hardware drivers and then use gnome-shell.

Greets.

### Post by aeronutt on 2011-10-05
> **Redsandro said:**
> ...

Indeed. How do you measure those amps so precisely? I can only measure the total wattage drain from my power outlett, like 150 amps. :P

Unplug laptop, then
```
more /proc/acpi/battery/BAT0/state
```
And get

present:                 yes
capacity state:          ok
charging state:          discharging
present rate:            821 mA
remaining capacity:      4371 mAh
present voltage:         12266 mV


Wouldn't know how to get an accurate reading on a desktop!

### Post by Redsandro on 2011-10-06
@**aeronutt**: I was intimidated by those crazy numbers, so I got quadruple boot now with Win7, Mint 11, Fedora 15 and Ubuntu 11.10.

Turns out, if you do that trick immediately, you don't get an accurate reading.

> $ uname -a
Linux Redsandro **2.6.38-8-generic #42-Ubuntu SMP** Mon Apr 11 03:31:24 UTC 2011 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
$ cat /proc/acpi/battery/BAT1/state 
present:                 yes
capacity state:          ok
charging state:          discharging
present rate:            **4755 mW**
remaining capacity:      46470 mWh
present voltage:         11858 mV

But if you keep cat'ting, you'll notice that consumption increases and increases and then stabilizes.
```
while true; do cat /proc/acpi/battery/BAT1/state; sleep 2s; done;
```

**Mint 11**/Gnome2+Compiz (2.6.38-8) went up to **16428 mW**
**Ubuntu 11.10 Beta2**/Unity (3.0.4) peaked at **16264 mW**
**Ubuntu 11.10 Beta2**/Gnome3-shell (3.0.4) peaked at **16326 mW**
**Fedora 15**/Gnome3-shell (2.6.40.4-5) peaked at **16175 mW**

See, differences are so small, can't even tell if it means anything or if it's just random.

This was measured on a Lenovo Thinkpad Edge.

### Post by sanderd17 on 2011-10-08
> **munkymac said:**
> I upgraded to 3.2 yesterday, and and tried to add the kagesensi pidgin extension (which was working perfectly on 3.0.2) via Gnome Tweak. It said the extension was added, prompted me to restart the shell, and then when it restarted, the extension wasn't listed in tweak. I checked in looking glass, and it shows that the extension is disabled, but without it showing up in Tweak, I'm not sure how to enable it. Thoughts?

I've seen if the metadata says it only supports Gnome 3.0, than it won't show up in the gnome tweak tool. This is because the Gnome Shell API changed quite a bit.

I do know that some guys got it working, but I don't know how. [https://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?pid=999525#p999525](https://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?pid=999525#p999525)
